## Task Management Software: What to Know Before You Buy

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/task-management-software/

**Contents:**
- Task Management Software: What to Know Before You Buy
- What Is Task Management Software?
- Types of Task Management Software
  - Standalone Task Management Tools
  - Project Management Software
  - Collaboration Platforms
  - Integrated Suites
- Features of Task Management Software
- Benefits of Task Management Software
- How to Choose the Best Task Management Tool

If you’re trying to become more organized, efficient, and—most importantly—ensure tasks don’t slip through the cracks, it’s time to consider task management software.

At the same time, you might be hesitant to adapt software to fit your strategy and make it work for you. You’re likely looking for an out-of-the-box solution—something you can start using right away, with minimal setup, to handle task management effortlessly.

You may also have specific requirements. For instance, you might want to integrate the task management tool with Microsoft Office, particularly Outlook, since email management plays a major role in your daily workflow.

Whether you’re part of a small team or a large organization, task management software can help streamline your workflow, enhance collaboration, and increase productivity. In this article, we’ll explore what task management software is, highlight its key features and benefits, and guide you on how to choose the right solution for your needs.

Task management software is a digital tool designed to help individuals and teams plan, organize, and track both group and individual tasks and projects. It allows users to centrally schedule duties, assign due dates, allocate responsibilities, and monitor progress.

By providing visibility into what’s being done, when it’s due, and who’s responsible, task management software helps ensure deadlines are met and projects stay on schedule.

Task management software comes in various forms, tailored to different needs and workflows. Below are the most common types:

These are single-purpose applications focused solely on task management. While they may offer fewer features than full project management platforms, they excel at simplifying task assignment and monitoring.

Ideal for small teams of 10 to 15 people, these tools typically don’t include production scheduling, reporting, or time tracking. Instead, they provide a simple, at-a-glance view of who’s working on what and what remains to be started.

Task management is often a core feature of broader project management tools. These platforms also include additional capabilities such as resource allocation, budgeting, and time tracking, making them suitable for managing complex projects from start to finish.

In platforms like Slack or Microsoft Teams, task management is often a built-in feature, allowing users to assign tasks within the context of team communication. These platforms support dynamic collaboration by integrating task tracking into real-time conversations.

End-to-end platforms like ClickUp and Asana offer comprehensive task management combined with features like document sharing, time tracking, and advanced reporting. These suites are designed to support the full workflow of a team, from planning through execution.

When selecting task management software, it’s important to identify which features best suit your team’s needs. Below are some of the most valuable and commonly sought-after features:

Task management software can transform the way your team operates. Here are some of the key benefits:

Choosing the right task management tool can be challenging. Here are some tips to help you decide:

Here are some popular task management tools suitable for different needs and budgets:

Investing in task management software is well worth it if you want to boost productivity, improve team management, and enhance organization. Every dollar spent can lead to greater efficiency and better outcomes from your employees. Take the time to assess your specific needs, business culture, and long-term goals as you evaluate options. With the right tool, you can transform how your team works and set them up for success.

Good luck with your technical writing!

Author, host, and deliver documentation across platforms and devices.

Task management software is a tool designed to help individuals and teams plan, organize, assign, and track tasks and projects efficiently. It centralizes task information and deadlines to improve visibility and ensure timely completion.

Common types include standalone task management tools, project management software with task features, collaboration platforms (like Slack or Microsoft Teams), and integrated suites (such as Asana or ClickUp) that combine multiple functionalities.

Identify your team’s needs and project types, prioritize ease of use, set a budget, check reviews, ensure strong customer support, verify integration options, look for customization capabilities, and confirm data security standards.

Yes, many tools offer real-time collaboration features like comments, file sharing, and notifications, which help keep remote or distributed teams connected and aligned.

Absolutely. Many task management tools are scalable and offer plans tailored to small teams, helping them stay organized and productive without complex setups.

Look for software with industry-standard security measures such as encryption, two-factor authentication, regular backups, and compliance with data protection regulations.

## 15 Best Technical Writing Software and Tools for 2026

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/15-best-technical-writing-software-and-tools-for-2024/

**Contents:**
- 15 Best Technical Writing Software and Tools for 2026
- What Is Technical Writing?
- What Should You Look For in a Technical Writing Tool?
- Best Technical Writing Tools for 2026
  - 1. ClickHelp
  - 2. HelpSmith
  - 3. Document360
  - 4. MadCap Flare
  - 5. Paligo
  - 6. HelpNDoc

To create clear, consistent, and professional documentation, a technical writer needs not only a good understanding of the end user’s needs and a certain talent for writing but also specialized tools. With the right software, it’s much easier to focus on delivering clear and concise content since a lot of other tasks and processes are better organized, more structured, or even automated, thus letting you have more time to work on what’s really important.

There will be plenty of great options for that in 2026, ranging from all-inclusive suites to focused applications. This article provides an overview of the top technical writing software and tools available this year based on features, capabilities, and overall value. Whether you need help organizing ideas, managing large documentation projects, building a knowledge base, creating diagrams, publishing in multiple formats, or anything else, there is a solution to boost your productivity as a technical writer. Read on for a comparison of the leading choices to equip your technical writing toolkit.

Technical writing is, basically, the process of explaining how to use a technology to a non-expert user in simple terms. The goal of a technical writer depends on the idea or information they need to convey. They may be simply providing users with information about a product, process, or concept in a clear and concise manner. Or they may give clear and concise instructions on performing a task. They may also work to persuade readers to take specific actions or, on the contrary, prohibit certain behaviors or actions. All through written documentation, such as repair manuals, how-to guides, instruction manuals, and others.

When evaluating technical writing software, there are certain capabilities that can optimize workflows. Look for tools that offer features like content reuse, variables, conditional text, and CCMS integration.

Choosing a platform with these key features allows technical writers to simplify documentation processes and focus on quality content.

ClickHelp is a modern, cloud-based technical writing tool for teams.

It provides a powerful platform for creating and editing content, with support for various content types such as images, videos, links, tables, and more. Additionally, ClickHelp allows for the customization of the reader experience with ready templates, visual settings, and CSS.

Pricing: Starts at $185/month. Live demos and free trials are available.

HelpSmith is an all-in-one XML-based technical writing tool that allows creating various types of documentation, including CHM Help files, Web Help systems, and PDF manuals. It offers features like a full-featured word processor, screen capture and image annotation, control annotations for UI elements, conditional compilation capabilities, topic templates, and more.

Pricing: Ranging from $199 to $399, with a free trial available for users to test the product before purchasing.

Document360 is an AI-powered SaaS tool that specializes in knowledge base creation and management, providing a centralized platform for storing and sharing information.

Pricing: Available on request.

MadCap Flare is a powerful content management tool tailored for technical writers, communicators, and content developers. It stands out as an all-inclusive solution due to its ability to enhance work processes, support multi-channel publishing, and facilitate efficient collaboration among team members.

Pricing: Starts at $182 per month.

Paligo is an enterprise-grade cloud-based CCMS that lets technical writers create and publish technical documentation efficiently while focusing on usability, collaboration, content reuse at scale, multi-channel publishing capabilities, structured authoring practices, and version control mechanisms tailored for technical writing needs.

Pricing: Starts at $79 per month.

HelpNDoc is a valuable tool for technical writers due to its single-source authoring capabilities, ease of use with a WYSIWYG environment, customization features for document appearance, dynamic content loading capabilities, and more.

Pricing: Starts at $99 per month.

Confluence is a versatile tool for technical writers due to its capabilities in providing a centralized platform for documentation management, offering a user-friendly interface, content reuse through macros, customization with apps from the marketplace, and more.

Pricing: This varies depending on the number of team accounts and includes a Free plan for teams of up to 10 users and three paid options starting at $6.05 per user monthly.

Dr.Explain is a fast, comfortable, and efficient technical writing tool for creating user manuals, software help files, user guides, and instructions. Its integrated utility for capturing and analyzing program windows and screenshots simplifies the process of creating technical documentation while offering features like structured text editing, image annotation, collaboration support through the TiWri cloud platform, and version control mechanisms tailored to technical writing needs.

Pricing: Starts at $290 per month.

Author-it is a comprehensive solution for technical writers due to its effective features that simplify the authoring process, support multi-channel publishing, facilitate efficient collaboration among team members, and implement version control mechanisms designed to meet technical writing needs.

Pricing: Reach out to the Author-It sales team to discuss your options.

HelpScribble is a reliable tool to create various help file formats. It offers automated formatting and layout, and provides collaborative editing capabilities.

Pricing: Starts at $99.00 for a single-user license.

Help+Manual is a comprehensive solution for technical writers because it prioritizes efficiency, user enjoyment, multi-format publishing capabilities, customization options, collaboration support, and other features necessary to meet technical writing requirements.

Pricing: Starts at €498 for a per-seat license.

Microsoft Word is still one of the most popular choices among technical writers due to its versatility, advanced formatting options, ease of use, and cost-effectiveness. While it does not have specialized features tailored specifically for technical writing, as some dedicated tools do, its widespread availability and familiarity make it a reliable option for efficiently creating a variety of technical documentation.

Pricing: Microsoft Word is available for a one-time purchase of $159.99 or as a part of the entire Microsoft 365 for a yearly subscription that starts at $6.99.

Google Docs serves as a versatile tool for technical writers due to its collaborative features, accessibility options, integration capabilities with various applications like Grammarly, and a lot of other useful features. While it may lack some specialized features, Google Docs remains a popular choice for its ease of use, accessibility across devices, and effortless collaboration capabilities.

Pricing: There is a free version with basic features for everyone, as well as paid plans with additional storage and advanced features for businesses. The pricing for Google Workspace, which includes Google Docs, starts at $6.00 per user per month for the Basic plan.

FAR HTML is designed to help users quickly manipulate HTML, XML, ASCII text files, or author help projects.

Wiki.js is a versatile tool for technical writing due to its customization options, security features, ease of installation, powerful access control mechanisms, and collaboration support.

Pricing: Wiki.js is an AGPL-licensed open-source project.

By streamlining formats, minimizing repetitive tasks, and organizing content, the best technical writing platforms increase productivity. Standalone applications have their own advantages, but complete suites offer end-to-end documentation solutions. In 2026 and beyond, technical writers will have even more options to equip their toolkits and expedite documentation delivery thanks to ongoing innovation in AI-authoring and smart content management.

Good luck with your technical writing!ClickHelp TeamAuthor, host and deliver documentation across platforms and devices

It’s 2 AM, and you’re deep in a documentation sprint. Your team is launching a complex product next week, and every minute counts. You need to work fast and make no mistakes.

We know this all too well, and that’s exactly why we’re turning documentation from a tedious task into an easy process that actually supports your team’s work. Our December update takes just another chunk of weight off your shoulders.

We’re adding a new optional topic editor.

No training required — just open, write, and communicate. You need your new junior technical writer to start working as quick as possible? Well, they can do that immediately with an intuitive, welcoming writing environment — and learn the full-featured editor later.

Finally, you can make your whole company — including finance and HR — write all kinds of documentation in a single place, without using a multitude of tools; the documentation management just got a lot easier!

You’ve just decided to launch your documentation portal with a custom domain. What should be a straightforward process turns into a grueling marathon of technical hurdles. Hours slip by as you navigate certificate configurations, DNS settings, and endless tweaks. One misconfigured setting means starting over. Another requires a manual renewal that you’ll inevitably forget.

What started as a simple branding effort becomes a recurring technical headache that pulls your team away from actual productive work.

We’ve transformed domain setup into a set-it-and-forget-it process. From 2-24 hours of manual work to less than 30 minutes just watching the process.

Forget all the certificate headaches. We handle everything automatically. Let’s Encrypt certificate requires one-time setup and zero future maintenance.

You prefer your own certificate? No problem. We support custom certificates alongside our automated solution.

Technical documentation rarely fits into perfect, uniform sentence structures that easily communicates across every language you might need. One language’s elegant paragraph easily becomes a translation nightmare in another. Especially when translation tools force every sentence into a rigid subscript.

We’re giving translators the flexibility they’ve been craving. Preserve the meaning, not just the structure. Work with paragraphs, not just sentences.

Want to expand a single sentence into multiple, more nuanced sentences? Do it.

Need to compress a wordy section into a more compact, culturally-adapted explanation? Go ahead.

Translation is no longer about matching word counts or sentence structures. It’s about capturing the essence of your documentation, regardless of linguistic differences.

Publication icons now include visibility markers that let you instantly understand what type of publication you’re looking at — public, private, or restricted.

We’ve optimized how images load in your documentation. Now, no matter how heavily your topic is filled with images, it will load almost instantly.

No more waiting for heavy pages to render fully: the images will only load when they come into view as the user scrolls down the page, whether you’re on a smartphone or a desktop.

## Cloud vs On-Premise Documentation Management System

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/cloud-vs-on-premise-documentation-management-system/

**Contents:**
- Cloud vs On-Premise Documentation Management System
- What Is an On-Premise Document Management System?
- Benefits and Shortcomings of an On-Premise System
- What Is a Cloud DMS?
- Benefits and Purpose of Cloud Compared to On-Premise Systems
- Best Cloud Document Management Systems
- Conclusion

Every growing company will sooner or later face the problem of creating a document management system (DMS). For this, certain steps should be made, and the first one is to decide whether it will be an on-premise or a cloud-based document management system. Looks like a simple question, but the system will have to meet a set of requirements, and the main thing is that it should make the life of your company easier.

An on-premise system is a document management system that implies using its own corporate server and a firewall. An own server is used not only to store all the documents but to install the on-premise document management software with all the necessary documentation tools. It is an in-house solution that helps to minimize the risks of information leakage.

An on-premise system is a good option for a business with high cyber security awareness. Storing the information on the company server is safe as there will be no unauthorized access to the server room, or, at least, it will be harder for the intruder to get access to the information. To ‘hack’ the server, this person will have to pass through the company security system. So, strategic data will be protected.

The second advantage is the economy. At first glance, purchasing a server (or several servers) might look like a heavy burden on the company budget. On the other hand, if regarded as a long-term investment, it might be quite reasonable. With a server of your own, you will not have to make monthly payments to the provider, and, in a couple of years, you will find out that having a server is a great economy as buying a server is actually cheaper than paying for server space to the provider.

Another advantage is that a corporate server makes the company independent from the Internet. An on-premise DMS can operate even if there is no internet connection. You can always rely on your own server: all the authorized employees will have access to data and software documentation tools and proceed working even if the Internet is ‘down.’ Independence from the Internet means an uninterrupted work process and enhanced efficiency.

A major disadvantage of the on-prem system is that it requires physical space (a server room) to install hardware. It means that you will have to think of additional space in your office building where you can install cabinets (racks) for server equipment, UPS units, and ventilation equipment as servers are climate-sensitive. You will also need a video camera as the server room needs to be monitored by the video surveillance system (it is important for security). Besides, you will have to think about fire alarms and fire extinguishers.

Labor costs should be included as well. Having a server means an additional workload on your IT team. Don’t forget that every ‘man-hour’ must be paid, and the number of person-hours will grow as the servers will require regular maintenance and updating.

All the expenses related to the server room must be included in the cost estimate. The total sum will be the real cost of your own company server.

If, after all the necessary pre-calculations, your own server still looks too expensive and you are not ready to invest in it, a cloud-hosted DMS can solve the problem.

A cloud document management system implies buying server space from a provider and using it for your company’s needs.

In terms of operation, there is practically no difference from an in-house system. The online document management system will have the same user interface; the company documents will be stored on the server (or ‘cloud’) and accessed by users. The only thing is that the server will be located not in the same office building but probably in a different city or country.

One of the most attractive features of all cloud services is that they give businesses a quick ready-made solution. You no longer have to think about purchasing expensive hardware with high performance and a huge memory. All your information will be stored in the cloud, and all your work will now be ‘cloud computing.’

In the case of a cloud document management system, all the software documentation tools will be started remotely. It means saving a large amount of memory on the company’s PCs and increasing the speed of operation.

Remote access to data also means that your employees can practice teleworking. It can result in economizing the company budget on renting offices and premises. Besides, unlike in the case of an on-premise system where users can get access only from office PCs, a cloud system enables users to work from any gadget they like, a PC, a tablet, or a smartphone.

Another way to save money is to economize on installing DMS software. Due to the cloud system, you no longer need to install programs on each PC, paying for each program separately. It is enough to pay just once for the cloud version of the software you need.

In the case of a cloud DMS, your IT team will no longer have to work on updating and maintenance. All the programs will be automatically updated to the latest version in the cloud, and your IT specialists will not have to check and monitor the updates.

Another important thing is that the cloud server can be easily scaled if your company grows or ‘shrinks.’ You are no longer limited by the hard disks. The cloud will meet your demands, and additional server space will be provided.

The market offers a wide variety of cloud document management systems designed to meet the demand of the most ‘picky’ customers. Below are just some of the most popular ones.

No matter what cloud system appears to be more appealing to you, you have to weigh all pros and cons and use the trial period most of these resources offer.

The more employees and customers you have, the more documents your company generates. Company documentation (be that developer documentation, user manuals, or case deflection) cannot be just dumped in information silos. This way, the knowledge base will be lost. Documents have to be issued, commented on, edited, adjusted, and shared. A DMS can streamline this process. That is why sooner or later, all growing companies face the dilemma of choosing between an on-premise and a cloud DMS. No matter what choice you make, you will have to consider all factors, among which the most important is security and budget economy.

Good luck with your technical writing! ClickHelp Team Author, host and deliver documentation across platforms and devices

## The Role of Collaboration Strategy in Organizational Success

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/collaboration-strategy-in-organizational-success/

**Contents:**
- The Role of Collaboration Strategy in Organizational Success
- What Is a Collaboration Strategy?
- Why Is Collaboration Important?
- ClickHelp: Your Key to Effortless Teamwork
- Conclusion
- FAQ

Effective collaboration is essential for any organization that wants to succeed in today’s fast-paced environment. A well-defined collaboration strategy fosters not only teamwork but also productivity and creativity among employees. In this article, we will take a closer look at what a collaboration strategy is and give an overview of ClickHelp as a tool for achieving seamless collaboration.

A collaboration strategy is a well-defined approach to how team members interact, share information, and work together towards common goals. It serves as a guide to foster teamwork, ensure open communication, and boost productivity within an organization. This strategy outlines guidelines for communication, feedback, and workflow, ensuring all team members remain focused and engaged.

For a collaboration strategy to be effective, team roles and responsibilities must be clearly defined. This often involves appointing a collaboration lead or project manager to oversee the execution of the strategy. This person facilitates communication, ensures everyone understands their roles, and provides the necessary support and resources. They play a pivotal role in setting expectations, defining objectives, and monitoring progress toward common goals.

The strategy also emphasizes participation and partnership among team members. Engaging employees in developing the strategy fosters a sense of ownership and accountability. Regular meetings and feedback sessions can be scheduled to assess the effectiveness of the strategy and make adjustments as needed, creating a dynamic collaboration environment.

A successful collaboration strategy should be supported by the right tools and technologies that enable smooth communication and information sharing, such as project management software, shared document platforms, and real-time messaging applications. These tools enhance transparency, streamline workflows, and ensure all team members have access to the necessary information for effective collaboration.

A successful collaboration strategy requires clear leadership, active engagement from team members, and the right technology. By encouraging collaboration and setting clear rules, organizations can increase teamwork, innovation, and the achievement of their goals.

Collaboration is crucial for the overall effectiveness and success of an organization for several key reasons. In today’s interconnected work environment, collaboration among teams is not just a benefit but a necessity for achieving desired outcomes. Here’s why:

Ultimately, collaboration is essential for thriving in a competitive environment. It drives organizational success by enhancing problem-solving, increasing employee engagement, improving productivity, fostering innovation, building stronger relationships, promoting knowledge sharing, and aligning goals. ## Maximizing User Satisfaction: Improving Technical Documentation with User Feedback

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/maximizing-user-satisfaction-improving-technical-documentation-with-user-feedback/

**Contents:**
- Maximizing User Satisfaction: Improving Technical Documentation with User Feedback
- Understanding User Feedback
- Analyzing User Feedback
- Implementing Changes Based on User Feedback
- The Benefits of User Feedback
- Using ClickHelp to Gather and Analyze User Feedback
- Conclusion

If technical writing is your trade, you should know that it is not a one-way street. Technical writers collaborate with other stakeholders, such as managers, marketers, and developers. However, many writers tend to forget that they work primarily for the target audience. That is why the main factor in developing technical documentation is the opinion of the users.

To a very large extent, it is the users who actually shape and reshape the documentation. Tech writers should take into account their expectations about the product, the way they see it, and how they want it to be. After all, the main idea behind tech writing is to get the message across to the users so that they can quickly grasp the information and start working with the product immediately.

Practice has shown that clear and effective technical documentation is based on the user experience (UX) accumulated and analyzed in the various forms of user feedback.

This article will explain the importance of UX in technical writing, show its impact on the quality of technical documentation, and analyze the methods and tools used to collect and analyze feedback to ensure the success of the product release on the market.

UX data can be categorized based on product parameters, specifically how users interact with the product. Consequently, we can single out the following UX data categories:

User feedback covers the aspects listed above and more, but the primary concern is how to collect and analyze this information effectively.

For this end, various methods are available. The best practices of feedback retrieval are:

The methods above provide effective ways to gather user feedback and make appropriate adjustments to technical documentation.

The role of technical writers is to plan and develop content that meet the needs of the target audience.

The needs of the audience are the cornerstone of any tech writer’s work. It is the Alpha and Omega – one has to analyze the needs first and meet the needs in the end.

Collecting feedback is important to learn about the needs of the users. There are many tools available to analyze user feedback, and web analytics is considered the primary tool for this purpose.

Web analytics allows measuring different UX parameters. Today, Google Analytics, Adobe Analytics, or some other web analytics are available, so you can collect the basic information. The most popular metrics allow measuring the following aspects of user experience:

The mentioned metrics are primarily used for SEO purposes, but it’s important to note that they can also be used as indicators for making changes not only to the supporting documentation, but to the product itself.

For example, metrics may help you learn which pages are the most popular with the users and how long they stay on these pages. If a topic is popular but it takes users too long to read the information (they spend too much time on the page), it probably means that the topic has to be rewritten in a simpler language. The improvements may concern the wording: may be the language is too complicated for the users to understand, and you will have to rewrite the page.

Similarly, if a page has too much text, adding visuals such as screenshots, tables, charts, videos, and gifs can improve content comprehension and user engagement.

Let’s have look at the implementation of changes based on information from support tickets. This is a simple and direct way to get feedback from users. However, it doesn’t work in 100% of cases. This is mostly due to the fact that when addressing support, users don’t describe the whole experience with the documentation. They usually formulate the problem and ask for a solution, but they very rarely describe how they were looking for the information, what pages they visited, what links they used, etc. So, what we get in this case is just the top of the iceberg.

On the other hand, when users take the time to describe their experience in detail, there is a chance that we get the whole picture and can fix the problem. This is usually done by adding the necessary information on the page, the info that the users found hard to understand or to find.

Forum threads are harder to research than support tickets. This is why this type of feedback collection is less popular. But, in terms of detailed description of the problem, forums may be much more effective, as users tend to describe their experience in full. As a result, it will be easier for your team to implement changes based on forum threads research.

One more thing to mention is the importance of testing and iterating on changes. You cannot just make changes without first testing if they give the desired result. For this purpose, it is advisable to conduct a usability study.

A usability study implies the iteration of actions supposed to be done by users. It helps to understand whether following the steps will help users obtain the desired result.

A usability study will be more effective if you don’t involve too many participants. Five or ten will be enough. The main thing is that they belong to different groups interacting with the product – developers, writers, users (beginners or expert users), marketers, managers, etc.

This choice will make your statistics more representative and will give you better results than testing a large but non-representative group. You will get more data and will be able to implement changes correctly.

The main benefit of user feedback is that it helps to improve technical documentation. However, more benefits can be mentioned that can be achieved in the course of work. These are:

The benefits of user feedback go far beyond the advantages listed above. If used properly, the feedback data will open up new opportunities for your product.

ClickHelp is an online documentation management platform that comes equipped with a built-in feedback system. This allows users to provide feedback directly within the documentation.

For example, ClickHelp has a statistics feature that enables monitoring the statistics of a particular topic or the project in general. It is handy if you want to know the number of views a topic has received.

Another way of analyzing the efficiency of user documentation is by using the Yes/No widget. This is a built-in feedback mechanism that allows developers to ask users if their experience was positive or negative. The Yes/No widget, also known as thumbs up/down, is useful in cases like “Was this X helpful?” The widget allows quickly testing the users’ opinion and collecting feedback. However, the widget provides only two response values.

To get more detailed information on a particular topic that users dislike, you can use fill-in forms that pop up when users click the thumb-down button. This feature helps collect more feedback on a specific topic that needs improvement.

ClickHelp also provides detailed analytics and reporting tools that help in making more accurate adjustments to your technical documentation.

Developing technical documentation is an ongoing process. It cannot be considered finished after releasing the documents and making them available to readers. You have to continually make changes and publish updates to ensure that the documentation meets the needs of your customers.

Technical writers have to take into account the needs and demands of different types of readers who interact with the product support documentation. To analyze these needs, it is highly recommended to use web metrics, surveys, interviews, usability tests, support tickets, user forums, etc. All these methods will allow you to analyze the effectiveness of technical documentation and make more precise adjustments in the future.

Good luck with your technical writing! ClickHelp Team Author, host and deliver documentation across platforms and devices

## How to Craft Effective Technical Datasheets

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/how-to-craft-effective-technical-datasheets/

**Contents:**
- How to Craft Effective Technical Datasheets
- Understanding Your Audience
- Minimum Requirements of a Datasheet
  - Specifications
  - Tolerances
  - Application Notes
  - Compliance Information
- Clarity & Readability
  - Structured Formatting
  - Bullet Points

Imagine a small engineering company that designs and manufactures high-current, high-voltage power resistors for a variety of applications—dynamic braking, neutral earthing, load banks, and more. They need to create technical datasheets for their range of products. These datasheets will likely include descriptive text, an image, and a table listing available sizes, current ratings, and part numbers.

Where should they start when creating these documents? What tools should they use? Is desktop publishing software the right choice?

These are just a few of the common questions that arise when developing technical datasheets. Datasheets are essential tools in engineering and product design. They provide critical information about products, materials, or components, enabling engineers, designers, and procurement specialists to make informed decisions.

Yet many datasheets are overly complex, vague, or simply unhelpful. In this comprehensive guide, we’ll explore best practices for writing clear, concise, and effective datasheets that truly serve their purpose.

Before drafting a datasheet, it’s important to understand who the intended readers are. These readers can vary widely and may include:

EngineersElectrical engineers, design engineers, application engineers, technicians, and production specialists all require documentation tailored to their needs. While some information in a datasheet is discipline-specific, other details have broader relevance.Engineers primarily need technical and performance information to determine how a product will function within their designs. They look for specifications that support design decisions, such as compatibility with existing systems, material strength, or load ratings.

Product DesignersProduct designers seek information related to applications, compatibility, and design considerations that help ensure a product meets user demands. Their focus is often on usability, aesthetics, and overall user experience. Including application notes and best practices can help them understand how the product is typically used in real-world scenarios.

Procurement SpecialistsPurchasing professionals need details on pricing, availability, and compliance to make informed buying decisions. They are interested in cost implications, lead times, and any regulatory requirements that might impact procurement.

End UsersIn certain cases—especially with consumer devices—even end users may refer to datasheets. They might be curious about how to use the device effectively or learn more about its features and benefits. Including a section that addresses frequently asked questions or common challenges can make the datasheet more user-friendly.

By identifying and reflecting on the needs of these different user groups, you can tailor your datasheet to provide relevant, accessible information for each audience.

A datasheet must meet certain minimum requirements to remain informative and useful. Below are the most essential elements to include:

This section outlines the technical specifications of the product, including:

Clearly state dimensional or performance tolerances. These are crucial for engineers who need to know acceptable levels of deviation for size or function. Properly defined tolerances ensure compatibility and uniformity across applications.

Offer guidance on how to properly use the product. This can include:

Whenever possible, include information about regulatory compliance (e.g., RoHS, CE marking). This is useful for procurement specialists and end users who need to verify that products meet industry standards. Compliance information also enhances the product’s credibility and marketability.

Datasheets are essential when working with complex equipment, but they can often feel overwhelming—especially for beginners. Even experienced professionals may spend significant time sifting through lengthy documents to find specific information. So, how can we make datasheets easier for beginners while also improving efficiency for professionals?

One of the most common mistakes in datasheet writing is a lack of clarity. To enhance readability, consider the following techniques:

Datasheets vary widely—not just in quality, but also in content. Some are simple tables of physical and electrical specifications, while others are in-depth programmer’s guides. A helpful approach is to include a contents menu or index at the beginning of the document.

Use clear headings, subheadings, and logical divisions to organize the content. This structure allows readers to quickly find what they need without reading through long paragraphs. A well-organized datasheet should guide users smoothly through the information they’re looking for.

Present specifications, features, and instructions in bullet point form. This format saves space, increases scanability, and reduces cognitive load. For example, listing each specification with a separate bullet point avoids confusion and makes the information easier to absorb.

As the saying goes, “the devil is in the details.” Often, a critical note—like a group of pins being internally connected—is buried in a single vague sentence. Even experienced engineers can miss it, having to re-read it multiple times.

For beginners, this problem is even worse. Manufacturers should aim to explain concepts clearly at a conceptual level—not just with a diagram and a vague one-liner. While engineers may dislike writing long explanations, skipping them can waste users’ time and lead to costly misunderstandings.

Although technical language is often necessary, try to minimize jargon that could alienate less technical readers. Strive for a balance between technical accuracy and accessibility. If complex terms must be used, include a glossary at the end of the datasheet to explain difficult terms.

Datasheets have increasingly become tools for marketing and sales, often containing exaggerated or vague claims. Limitations, bugs, or discrepancies between documented and real-world performance are frequently omitted.

The best approach is to use clear, plain language. Avoid unnecessary words and focus on delivering the most relevant information. For example, instead of saying, “The product is intended to be used in various applications,” simply write, “The product is suitable for various applications.” It’s shorter, clearer, and more informative.

Visual elements play a crucial role in making a datasheet more effective and user-friendly. Here’s how to use them well:

Tables are excellent for presenting specifications, tolerances, and performance data in a clear, structured format. They allow users to view and compare multiple attributes at once. For example, a side-by-side comparison table showing different versions of a product helps clients quickly determine which option best suits their needs.

Graphs are ideal for displaying performance metrics over time or under varying conditions, making trends and capabilities easier to understand. For instance, a graph showing performance versus temperature can be extremely valuable to product designers evaluating reliability in different environments.

Product images or diagrams provide context and help users visualize how the product fits into their application. Labels should be clear and cross-referenced in the main text. For example, if you include a diagram of the product’s components, explain in the text what each part does and how it relates to the overall function.

For complex processes, flowcharts can be used to illustrate product operation, troubleshooting steps, or repair procedures. They simplify complicated workflows, making them easier to understand and follow—especially helpful for end users and technicians.

Even experienced professionals can make mistakes when creating datasheets. Below are some of the most common pitfalls—and how to avoid them:

Creating effective technical datasheets requires a strong focus on the reader, clear organization of key information, and a commitment to clarity and readability. By using a structured layout, incorporating visuals, and avoiding common pitfalls, you can produce datasheets that truly serve their purpose—delivering useful, actionable information to engineers, product designers, and procurement professionals.

Good luck with your technical writing!

Author, host and deliver documentation across platforms and devices

A technical datasheet provides clear, concise, and accurate information about a product’s specifications, performance, and usage. It serves as a reference for engineers, designers, procurement specialists, and sometimes end users to make informed decisions.

The key audiences include engineers (e.g., electrical, design, or application engineers), product designers, procurement specialists, and occasionally end users. Each group looks for different types of information relevant to their roles.

Use structured formatting, clear headings, bullet points, and plain language. Avoid excessive jargon, and ensure content is logically organized and easy to scan.

Include tables for specs, graphs for performance data, diagrams or illustrations for context, and flowcharts for complex processes. Visuals help simplify complex information and improve user understanding.

Avoid overly technical language, missing or incomplete data, inconsistent formatting, and neglecting reviews. Always seek feedback post-release to refine the datasheet based on real user input.

While their primary purpose is technical communication, datasheets can support marketing by showcasing key features and compliance credentials. However, they should remain honest, accurate, and avoid exaggerated claims.

## Create a Technical Manual - Types of Technical Manuals

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/how-to-create-a-technical-manual/

**Contents:**
- How to Create a Technical Manual
- What Is a Technical Manual?
- Why Companies Invest in Technical Manuals
- Types of Technical Manuals
  - User Manuals
  - Installation Manuals
  - Maintenance and Service Manuals
  - Repair Manuals
  - Operations Manuals
  - Troubleshooting Guides

Product success in today’s extremely competitive market no longer depends solely on features or functionality. Technical documentation, though often overlooked, is one of the most influential factors in whether a product is accepted or rejected. Technical manuals are critical resources that provide users with the necessary knowledge for proper operation, maintenance, and troubleshooting. Everything from simple user guides to detailed API references or troubleshooting manuals is essential in delivering a good user experience, reducing support costs, and maintaining product safety and compliance.

A technical manual serves as a comprehensive document that offers instructions, guidelines, and detailed explanations about a product, system, or process. These manuals are designed to assist users, whether they are end-users, technicians, engineers, or developers, in understanding and operating a product effectively. They help prevent misuse, ensure compliance with safety standards, and provide solutions for potential issues.

Let’s explore the different types of technical manuals, why companies invest in them, and their significant impact on customer satisfaction and business success.

Companies invest in technical manuals because they are crucial to the overall success of their products or services. Technical manuals are not one-page instructions; rather, they are strategic tools with significant effects on customer satisfaction, brand reputation, and operational efficiency.

In summary, technical manuals are an investment with numerous benefits, including improved customer satisfaction, reduced support costs, assured safety and compliance, enhanced innovation, strengthened brand reputation, and more effective employee training. Through these advantages, technical manuals help companies maintain market competitiveness.

When creating a technical manual, it is important to note that there are many types of this document, each serving different purposes and users. These include:

An overview of these types can assist in tailoring the manual to meet the specific needs of its users.

User manuals, sometimes referred to as instruction manuals, outline the procedures for using or operating a product or system. Typically, these documents include step-by-step instructions, descriptions of potential issues, precautions to take while using the product, and maintenance guidelines. The primary audience for user manuals is non-technical users who rely on these documents to provide detailed explanations of all procedures to maximize a product’s value.

Examples include user guides for smartphones, instructions for home appliances, and software installation guides.

Installation guides provide detailed instructions for setting up and installing hardware or software products. These manuals are typically aimed at technicians or other professionals responsible for ensuring correct installation. As a result, the content can be more technical and may include diagrams, wiring schematics, and system requirements.

Common examples include network equipment installation guides, software deployment manuals, and machinery setup instructions.

These manuals are typically used by technicians and engineers for servicing, troubleshooting, and repairing equipment. They usually contain detailed technical information such as circuit diagrams, parts lists, maintenance schedules, and repair procedures. The primary goal is to keep the product in good working order and to effectively service it when it fails or malfunctions.

Examples include automobile repair manuals, industrial machinery maintenance guides, HVAC system service manuals, and many others.

Repair manuals focus specifically on troubleshooting and fixing problems in a product or system. These guides provide detailed instructions for disassembling, repairing, and reassembling a product’s components. They are generally used by repair technicians or advanced users who want to understand exactly how a product works and how to fix it when something goes wrong.

Examples include model-specific automotive repair guides, electronics repair guides, appliance repair handbooks, and more.

Operations manuals contain information on running a system or plant equipment effectively and safely. They are primarily used in industrial, manufacturing, or technical settings and are intended for operators who need to be well-versed in the standard operating procedures. These manuals typically include guidelines on operating procedures, safety protocols, and steps to be taken during emergencies.

Examples include operation manuals for factory equipment, aircraft flight operations, and laboratory equipment.

Troubleshooting guides help users diagnose common problems and provide solutions as quickly as possible. These guides typically include a list of symptoms or error messages, potential causes, and steps to resolve the issues. They often use a question-and-answer format or flowchart to guide users through the problem-solving process. Troubleshooting guides are valuable tools for both end-users and support technicians, as they help reduce downtime and prevent unnecessary repairs.

Typical examples of troubleshooting guides include computer software problem-solving guides, printer troubleshooting manuals, and guides for home appliances.

API documentation is a type of technical writing that describes how to interface with different systems. It typically includes descriptions of all available API endpoints, parameter definitions, authentication methods, request and response formats, and example code snippets. Well-written documentation allows developers to quickly understand how to interact with the API, facilitating integration and preventing misuse.

Examples include REST API documentation for web services, GraphQL API reference guides, payment gateway API documentation, and more.

An SDK comes with documentation that helps developers effectively utilize its tools, libraries, and code. This documentation typically includes installation guidelines, setup instructions, code examples, and best practices. SDK documentation is crucial in the app-building or feature-integration process.

Examples include guides for SDKs in mobile app development, SDK documentation for gaming engines, and cloud services SDK manuals.

Release notes are a comprehensive summary of all updates, enhancements, bug fixes, and new features included in a new version of a product or software. They are typically intended for end-users, developers, and stakeholders who need to be informed about the changes in a release.

Common examples include update notes for software versions and update summaries for mobile apps in app stores.

Release notes usually include the following elements:

A well-crafted set of release notes helps communicate with users, allowing them to understand the reasons for updating and how to transition to the new version with minimal effort.

Policy and procedure manuals outline the standards, policies, and procedures of an organization, typically related to internal processes, safety, or compliance. These manuals are essential for ensuring that employees have a comprehensive understanding of the organization’s rules and guidelines and follow the correct procedures in their daily tasks.

Examples include human resources policy manuals, safety procedure guides, and corporate compliance handbooks.

A reference manual is a source document that provides detailed information on a specific subject, product, or system. This type of documentation is typically aimed at professionals who need to frequently look up information or data. It may include technical specifications, code libraries, or detailed explanations of concepts and functions.

Typical examples of reference manuals include API documentation for software developers, engineering reference guides, and medical reference handbooks.

Different types of technical manuals serve various purposes and audiences. When developing your manual, it is important to identify the type that most closely aligns with your product or system to ensure the content meets the requirements and expectations of its users.

Below is a table outlining the different types of technical manuals and their primary purposes.

While creating a technical manual may seem daunting at first glance, a proper approach can help you develop a comprehensive and user-friendly guide that effectively communicates complex information. This blog provides an overview of the steps necessary to create a technical manual, from initial planning to final publication.

Before you start writing a technical manual, it’s important to understand its purpose and intended audience. Clearly identify what the manual is expected to achieve. For example, will it teach readers how to use a product, install software, troubleshoot problems, or provide maintenance instructions?

Consider the level of your readers. Are they novices, average users, or professionals? Tailor the information to their level of expertise.

The next step is to gather all the necessary information and structure it into a step-by-step process. Collect information from SMEs and review any available materials. If necessary, test the product or software yourself to gain firsthand experience.

Create a well-structured outline that includes all sections and subsections of your manual. A typical outline might include:

With an outline in place, you can begin drafting the content. Here are some basic tips:

Editing the document is crucial. There are several methods to ensure accuracy, consistency, and clarity:

The manual should be tested to ensure it effectively instructs users on how to use the product before publication. This process is known as usability testing. Have someone who is unfamiliar with the product work through the manual step by step. Observe any problems or areas of confusion, and revise the content as needed.

Once the manual has undergone sufficient review and testing, it’s time to publish and distribute it. Choose the format that best suits your target audience—whether it’s a printed booklet, a PDF document, or an online guide. Ensure that it is accessible and easy to use.

Distribute the manual to your users through various means, such as including it with your product, making it downloadable from your website, or sending it via email.

A technical manual is not a one-time project. Just as products and software evolve, so should the documentation. Therefore, maintain a feedback loop by allowing users to provide comments on how the manual could be improved. This will help identify areas that need clarification or correction.

Periodic reviews of the manual are essential to ensure it remains current with changes in the product, software updates, and new user feedback.

By following these steps, you can develop a manual that performs its function effectively while enhancing the user experience with your product.

A well-written technical manual is an invaluable tool for helping users get the most out of your offering, reducing support needs, and reinforcing your brand’s reputation for quality and dependability.

To streamline the creation of high-quality technical manuals, consider using ClickHelp. ClickHelp is an exceptional tool designed specifically for managing comprehensive, user-friendly documentation. Its features, such as collaborative editing, advanced publishing options, and integrated feedback mechanisms, make it ideal for producing technical manuals that meet all your documentation needs. ClickHelp’s intuitive interface and robust functionality can significantly enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of your technical writing process, ensuring your manuals are as effective and professional as possible.

Good luck with your technical writing!

Author, host, and deliver documentation across platforms and devices.

Technical manuals help users operate, install, maintain, and troubleshoot products, ensuring a smooth user experience and reducing support costs.

There are various types, including user manuals, installation guides, maintenance manuals, repair manuals, operations manuals, API documentation, and more.

A good manual requires understanding the audience, gathering accurate information, organizing content effectively, using clear language, incorporating visuals, and conducting thorough reviews.

Technical manuals should be updated whenever a product changes, new features are added, or user feedback indicates the need for improvements.

Formats vary based on audience needs, including print, PDFs, interactive web documents, and embedded help systems.

## 5 Simple Steps to Build a Career in Technical Writing

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/5-simple-steps-to-build-a-career-in-technical-writing/

**Contents:**
- 5 Simple Steps to Build a Career in Technical Writing
- 1. Practice
- 2. Consider Open-Source Projects
- 3. Stay in the Loop
- 4. Get Certification
- 5. Differentiate Yourself from Other Technical Writers
- Over to You

So, you’re considering a career in technical writing. Maybe we’re a bit biased, but that’s an excellent choice. You’ll get to work closely with the products and services that may change people’s lives and prepare user documentation for manuals, articles, online help and other supporting documents. It’s a gratifying job, though not without challenges.

Here are some of the challenges you will face:

But, the benefits outweigh the disadvantages:

There are a lot of companies out there looking for skilled technical writers to help them create their knowledge base – and, we’re not referring just to the IT industry.

Here’s a list of five things that can help set yourself apart…

Technical writing is, at its core, dependent on good writing skills. Now, you can’t exactly learn how to write well, but with a decent amount of practice, your skills can improve significantly.

Once you get the hang of things, you should consider publishing your work. Start a blog dedicated to tutorials in the industry you want to work in, and slowly build a following. It’s a great form of practice and something you can use to your advantage at an interview.

You may also like to read about the 11 essential skills of a good technical writer.

Just because you’re not in the industry yet, doesn’t mean you can’t acquire experience working in it. Open-source projects are in high need of user documentation. Look for something that is relevant to your skills and contribute to it.

A job in technical writing requires a lot of research. You’re not all knowledgeable, and you need to study a lot to be able to communicate the desired message to the target audience. But, if you want to set yourself apart, then you should go the extra mile and try to keep up to date with the latest trends in your niche. That way, you can become an expert and have an easier time acquiring projects.

Although not mandatory, a diploma in the industry you’re interested in or even certification in writing or communication can give you a great competitive advantage. There are a lot of classes you can take online that can help you practice your skills and acquire more knowledge.

Technical writing can be a pretty competitive industry, so you need to show employers that you can do more than just write quality content. For instance, if you want to work in a software company, you could also learn to code. That way, you’ll be able to understand the service better and create a fantastic knowledge base. You should also become knowledgeable in using help authoring tools to gain an advantage over other writers.

Regardless if you opt to work as a freelancer, or you decide to join a company full time, use these tips to build your portfolio before you start sending your resume.

Good Luck with your technical writing! ClickHelp Team Online Technical Writing & Documentation Tools

## ClickHelp December 2025 Release: WriteAssist Joins AI Suite

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/clickhelp-december-2025-release/

**Contents:**
- ClickHelp December 2025 Release: WriteAssist Joins AI Suite
- WriteAssist Levels Up: Context-Aware Editing Joins AI Suite
- AnswerGenius Custom Prompts: Make It Speak Your Language
- Other Improvements
  - AnswerGenius API: One License, Full Access.
  - AnswerGenius Greetings Message Control
  - Source Mode Version Comparison
  - Work with Bigger Files: 1GB File Upload Limit
  - Streamlined Navigation: Ask Your Docs in Tools Menu
- Ready to unwrap these features?

We couldn’t leave you without presents, so we’ve prepared our December update just in time for the holidays. This release brings WriteAssist into the AI Suite with important improvements, makes AnswerGenius customization publicly available, and adds several other conveniences.

WriteAssist now analyzes the entire documentation structure, including parent, sibling, and child topics, rather than just individual paragraphs. Suggestions now align with the broader context of your project.

Refined prompts ensure WriteAssist maintains your specific brand voice.

Also, WriteAssist has moved to the CAPs payment model as part of the AI Suite, joining AnswerGenius with shared credits. Monitor your usage for both WriteAssist and AnswerGenius on a single subscription page.

You can now define how AnswerGenius interacts with your users. Custom prompts allow you to:

Imagine a healthcare documentation portal where users type “BP” and AnswerGenius immediately knows they mean “blood pressure,” not “business process.” This is just one example of how custom prompts help AnswerGenius understand your domain and communicate more effectively.

AnswerGenius API access no longer requires a separate API license. If you have an AI Suite, you can integrate AnswerGenius into your applications immediately.

Whether you’re building a help widget for your SaaS product, embedding smart search in your company intranet, or creating a custom support interface, you can now do it with just your existing AI Suite subscription.

You can now disable AnswerGenius’s automatic greeting when users open the chat. Some prefer a minimalist interface without automatic messages, and now they have that choice.

Contributors can now compare topic versions in Source mode, seeing every change in the underlying HTML and code. Readers still see Design mode comparisons, but Contributors get the detailed view they need for precise change tracking.

This is particularly valuable for teams maintaining strict markup standards or working with complex custom styling.

We’ve increased the maximum file size to 1GB for both File Manager and API uploads. Whether you’re uploading a detailed product demo video or a complete asset archive for your design team, keep everything centralized in ClickHelp.

A small but logical UX improvement: Ask Your Docs has moved to the Tools menu in your left navigation panel. This change makes the interface more intuitive — all your utility features are now in one predictable location.

All these improvements are available now in the December 2025 release. Start a free trial to refine your documentation with context-aware WriteAssist, or customize AnswerGenius for your industry.

Here’s to closing 2025 with tools that truly understand your work!

Good luck with your technical writing!ClickHelp TeamAuthor, host and deliver documentation across platforms and devices.

## International Blogger Day in Technical Writing

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/international-blogger-day-in-technical-writing/

**Contents:**
- International Blogger Day in Technical Writing
- Conclusion

Today, June 14, is the International Blogger Day. Or, is it? Turns out there is no official blogger day, but it seems that in 2004 some people did choose this day to celebrate their passion for blogging! And, for the lack of a better date, we decided to join them, because, you know what? Blogging alongside podcasts, conferences and the like, is a big part of modern technical communication.

For some people, blogging is not just a hobby, but something they make a living from. In case of technical writers, blogging is rather a side hustle than something serious, but the amount of value techcomm bloggers bring is crazy.

Getting started on a new career path can stress you out and get your head spinning with information. But, don’t worry, there are dozens of techcomm-related blogs full of vital information and valuable insights waiting for you on the Internet. We would like to thank everyone who is blogging about help authoring today – you are helping the community a lot and leaving behind great heritage for the new generations of technical writers.

Here’s our list of technical communicators who can be also called bloggers:

This technical writer doesn’t need an introduction. Ugur has been multitasking for a while in the techcomm community: he has been consistently updating his blog, doing public speaking, answering questions on technical writing on Quora, has put up several online courses and even has a techcomm YouTube channel. Ugur Akinci is many things and a blogger is one of them. His content is always on point and we highly recommend you check it out!

Here comes another big name in technical communication – Tom Johnson. His blog on technical writing trends and innovations is huge in the community of technical writers and it has been for a long time. Many bloggers are trying themselves in different roles and Tom Johnson is no exception: he has been into podcasts since forever which feels like a nice addition to the blog.

A techcomm specialists with 40 years of experience in help authoring. Larry Kunz is also teaching product management in the Technical Communication certificate program at Duke University. Larry is one of the tech writers who witnessed the evolution of technical communication and he speaks about that on his blog, too. Younger technical writers sure have a lot to learn from this expert. Larry Kunz writes about style guides, gives professional advice to fellow writers, and, at times, his blog gets quite personal – he gives his perspective on many things that are happening worldwide.

Rhonda started her ‘cyber consulting’ in 2001 with a quarterly newsletter. By 2008 her audience grew to the point where the newsletter didn’t suffice anymore, so she started a blog. Rhonda’s blog is quite technical and very practice-oriented – she posts tips and how-to’s, writes about style, gives book recommendations and many, many more.

Aerospace is one of the major industries that employ technical writers. If you always wanted to reach for the stars (literally!), but were too afraid to try because you majored in journalism or literature and felt like the Jon Snow of NASA (knew nothing about aerospace), you should definitely visit Bart Leahy’s blog.

He can give real advice on how to get hired by NASA! Plus, Bart posts things like lists of space terms and acronyms, space-related book recommendations, inspirational articles on staying motivated, tackles freelancing on multiple occasions, etc. It is safe to say that even if you are not really into aerospace technical writing, you still might find his blog very useful.

TechWhirl is a big portal for technical writers on content management and technical communications. And, it has its own blog with a plethora of authors there. Let’s name some of the bloggers:

These people cover the biggest topics on techcomm regularly and we are grateful for such a nice resource of free knowledge!

We are living in a beautiful era with so much information easily available. But, we desperately need people who would gather it and structure it for us, rework it and explain how to apply it. That’s what techcomm bloggers do and more. They introduce new concepts and ideas, share their own findings and lifehacks, make us laugh at times (judging by the blogs, most of them have a fine sense of humor!) and serve as the ultimate source of knowledge.

Bloggers of the technical writing world, thank you!

Good luck with your technical writing! ClickHelp Team Author, host and deliver documentation across platforms and devices
