## How Do I Create a Table of Contents in Word

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/how-do-i-create-a-table-of-contents-in-word/

**Contents:**
- How Do I Create a Table of Contents in Word
- Why You Need a Table of Contents in Word
- How a Table of Contents Works in Word
- How to Insert a Table of Contents in Word
- Creating Subsections in Your Table of Contents
- Adjusting Your Table of Contents
- Updating Your Table of Contents When Necessary
- Using the Navigation Pane on a Side Table of Contents in Word
- Managing a Table of Contents in a Collaborative Word Document
- ClickHelp: Table of Contents Features

A Table of Contents (TOC) in Microsoft Word helps readers navigate long documents by listing headings with page numbers. Below you’ll learn how to insert, create subsections, customize, and update a TOC in Word—and how the ClickHelp TOC solves multi-author and web-publishing needs.

A table of contents is helpful because it:

• Structures your document with clear headings and sections for easier reading.

• Lets readers jump to important topics instantly.

• Keeps long reports, manuals, or training materials organized.

• Automatically reflects changes when you edit headings or pagination.

Word builds the TOC from built-in Heading styles (Heading 1, Heading 2, Heading 3). Apply these styles to the paragraphs you want included. Word then creates a formatted list with numbers and links that update as needed.

Tip: Manual formatting (bold, font size) doesn’t count. Use the Styles gallery (Home tab → Styles).

Working with large Word documents can be disorienting, so adding a table of contents makes navigation much easier and gives your document a polished, professional look. Here’s how to add one in both the desktop and web versions of Microsoft Word.

For the desktop version of Word:

1. Open the Word document where you want to insert the table of contents.

2. Go to the References tab on the top ribbon.

3. Select Table of Contents.

4. Choose an automatic style, such as Automatic Table 1 or Automatic Table 2.

Word will then generate a table listing all the headings in your document that use Word’s built-in heading styles. The table includes page numbers and automatically updates as you make changes to your document.

1. Open your Word document.

2. Click the References tab on the ribbon.

3. Click Table of Contents.

4. Click Insert Table of Contents.

This also creates a navigable list of your document’s headings.

Using these features saves time and allows readers to move easily between sections without manually editing entries. Be sure that your headings are formatted with Word’s heading styles so the table populates correctly.

A table of contents is especially useful for organizing long documents such as reports, manuals, or training guides. With just a few clicks, you can create a dynamic, automatically updating table that keeps your work professional and easy to navigate.

To include subheadings (or nested headings) in your table of contents, you need to format them with lower-level heading styles such as Heading 2, Heading 3, and so on. Microsoft Word automatically recognizes these styles and indents them beneath the main headings (usually Heading 1), creating a clear visual hierarchy in your table of contents.

This structure helps readers understand your document’s layout at a glance — from broad sections down to detailed subsections — keeping everything neatly organized.

In practice, apply Heading 1 to main titles, Heading 2 to their subtopics, and Heading 3 for any further breakdowns. This cascading formatting not only improves navigation but also gives your document a more professional appearance. Word will automatically adjust the indentation and hierarchy whenever you modify or add headings, so you don’t have to update the table of contents manually.

Consistently applying heading styles and dividing content into subsections is essential for producing a clear, accessible table of contents in Word.

Microsoft Word lets you easily adjust and customize your table of contents (TOC) at any time, giving you full control over how your document’s structure appears and functions.

To adjust a pre-existing TOC:

Go to the References tab, click Table of Contents, and then select Custom Table of Contents.

Word uses built-in heading styles (Heading 1, Heading 2, and so on) to determine what appears in the TOC. If you’re comfortable with field codes, you can further refine which heading levels or custom styles are included by editing the TOC field directly.

By taking advantage of these tools, you can ensure your table of contents perfectly reflects your document’s structure and visual style, making it both easy to navigate and professionally presented.

Whenever you add, remove, or rename headings in your document, you should update the table of contents to reflect those changes.

Word will automatically refresh your table of contents to match the current structure of your document.

If you want a side-placed, clickable outline of your document, Microsoft Word’s Navigation Pane is the most effective tool to use. It acts as a live, interactive map of your document’s headings, letting you jump instantly to any section — perfect for working with long documents.

Here’s how to use it:

Using the Navigation Pane alongside a traditional table of contents gives you both a dynamic, side-based navigation system while editing and a polished, clickable TOC for readers. Remember, consistent use of heading styles is what makes both tools work perfectly together.

Creating and updating a Table of Contents (TOC) in a collaborative Word document requires careful coordination to maintain organization and professionalism. A common issue arises when multiple authors modify headings manually—by changing font size, bolding, or adding spacing—instead of using Word’s built-in heading styles (Heading 1, Heading 2, Heading 3). This can prevent Word from recognizing headings properly, causing the TOC to omit entries or appear jumbled.

To avoid this, all contributors should consistently apply the proper heading styles throughout the document. You can verify usage by opening the Styles window in Word and checking which paragraphs are assigned to each heading. If inconsistent styles have already been applied, they can be corrected retroactively; what matters most for the TOC is the underlying style, not the exact visual formatting.

Once heading styles are standardized, Word’s dynamic TOC automatically updates as sections are added, removed, or changed. Clear guidance and coordination among contributors ensure that the TOC accurately reflects the document’s structure, providing readers with an effective navigation tool and keeping the final assembly process smooth.

For teams managing large documentation projects, ClickHelp provides a highly dynamic and collaborative Table of Contents (TOC) system. Unlike Word, which relies on heading styles, ClickHelp’s TOC is a hierarchical tree that organizes content in a flexible and interactive way.

Key features include:

ClickHelp’s TOC system supports multi-author collaboration, with role-based permissions, real-time updates, and commenting features that let teams coordinate effectively. Contributors can work simultaneously without conflicting edits, and readers can rely on a consistent, structured, and easily navigable documentation experience.

This makes ClickHelp especially valuable for complex manuals, knowledge bases, or any documentation project where clear structure and collaborative efficiency are essential.

Creating a table of contents in Microsoft Word helps you organize your document effectively and allows readers to navigate information quickly. By consistently using heading styles, inserting an automatic TOC, customizing its appearance, and updating it as your document evolves, you can enhance both the professionalism and readability of your work.

For online documentation, tools like ClickHelp offer advanced features for managing a hierarchical, interactive TOC, supporting multi-author collaboration, quick navigation, and real-time updates. Whether you are working in Word or using specialized documentation software, a well-structured table of contents is essential for creating clear, accessible, and professional documents.

Good luck with your technical writing!

Author, host and deliver documentation across platforms and devices.

Go to References → Table of Contents → Automatic Table. Make sure your headings use built-in Heading styles.

Same steps: select the TOC location → References → Table of Contents → choose an automatic style, then update as needed.

In Word (desktop), use References; in Word Online, open References → Table of Contents → Insert Table of Contents.

Apply Heading 1/2/3 to your document sections, then insert an Automatic Table from the References tab.

Use styles for headings → References → Table of Contents → pick a built-in layout → Update when content changes.

While ChatGPT proved valuable for tasks such as content creation and customer support automation, some companies began exploring additional AI tools to complement and enhance their workflows. These businesses were looking for alternatives that offered greater flexibility, customization, and security.

In response to these needs, companies began integrating ChatGPT with other AI tools that better aligned with their specific goals. This approach allowed them to leverage the strengths of multiple platforms, improving performance, personalization, and data privacy. While ChatGPT is a powerful tool, businesses may seek alternatives due to network overload, limited free requests, or the need for more specialized functionality. Some companies require more customization or specific integrations, which may be difficult to achieve with ChatGPT alone. Additionally, data privacy concerns and user experience differences may drive businesses to explore other AI solutions that better align with their specific needs.

Businesses often encounter slow response times or outages due to high traffic, especially during peak usage. The limited free requests can also hinder experimentation, while the generalist nature of ChatGPT might not meet the specific needs of tasks like technical documentation or customer support. Paid AI tools offer additional features like higher usage limits, better accuracy, and access to the latest models such as GPT-4. While free tools like ChatGPT are suitable for basic tasks, paid solutions provide businesses with scalability, reliability, and support that are necessary for growth.

## 2021 Trends in Technical Writing and Software Documentation

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/2021-trends-in-technical-writing-and-software-documentation/

**Contents:**
- 2021 Trends in Technical Writing and Software Documentation
- More User Guides!
- More Cloud-Based Services and SaaS!
- More User-Focused Documentation!
- More Standards in Documentation!
- Mobile Use!
- Conclusion

As everyone says today, 2020 has drastically changed everything: our private lives, hobbies, relationships, habits, and careers. Technical writing is no exception. Unexpectedly, the pandemic has brought so much new in this sphere that we all need to figure out what is going to be a trend next year.

Yes, in 2021, we are going to write even more user guides. Look around – everything has gone online. There are so many tools and services to buy food, study, work from home, keep in touch, etc. Software is developing as fast as never before! Artificial intelligence, augmented reality, virtual reality, and many other technologies are here to bring our everyday routine to the next level. The thing is that recently, the quality of images has become so high that one can see even the smallest details of an item. That is amazing when you shop online. You can see the real colors, texture, and materials. Some applications even allow users to view items in 3D and rotate them. To say nothing of the quality of infographics! This year, the maps and diagrams showing the influence of the pandemic became one of the main sources of information. They are very detailed and precise. The information is updated nearly every hour. Besides, such spheres as robotics and drone production are waiting for their time to come. That could be a great solution to use them to replace humans where it is possible – for example, in delivery services – to limit contacts.

All those technologies, tools, and services are created for users. And, someone has to provide them with perfect user manuals and training documentation.

So, technical writers are going to have a lot of work with software documentation in the nearest future. We all expect this sphere to flourish in 2021.

This year, when we had to work from home a lot, we saw that cloud-based services and SaaS are a must-have for those who want to adapt to the new reality and stay productive. They allow users to work from any place and cooperate successfully with each other without losing relevant data. Only the Internet connection is required. Another trend here is simplicity. All those tools must be easy-to-use and clear to everyone. Of course, technical writers are smart enough to master any new tool, but we all appreciate, first of all, intuitive use.

Thousands of technical writers have already started to use online documentation tools, and many more are coming to save and improve their workflow. In ClickHelp, we are happy to let them work efficiently. We both create a feature-rich environment to write content and make the teamwork smoother. To say nothing of review and import/export processes.

Our goal is to make the tech writing routine as easy as possible.

Choosing a technical documentation tool make sure it meets all your expectations and has all the features to improve your workflow.

At the moment, the focus has shifted to users and how they interact with a software product. First and foremost, documentation should be accessible and easy-to-find. It should have a clear structure and allow users to find what they are looking for. And, finally, the content should be focused on their pains and support the users’ decision-making. Several weeks ago, we had a good post about that – Best Tips to Create Valuable Software Documentation. With the arrival of numerous software products, this trend is going to be on top.

This trend comes from the fact that more and more software products are aimed at the international market, not the local one. So, documentation is supposed to be written according to numerous international standards as it should be easy-to-understand for all users. The use of a specific language – Simplified Technical English (STE) will only increase as the majority of users are not native speakers. The following post will give you more information – What is Simplified Technical English.

Another peculiar tendency is that help authoring tools offer users translation and localization services to speed up document creation. The ClickHelp Team is working on that too, and next year we are going to see the first results. Of course, machine translation cannot fully substitute human translation. At least, at the moment. Using the inbuilt translation services, a company will need not a translator but an editor. And, definitely, automated localization will save so much time and effort. Tech writers who create documentation for the international market know, like no one else, that even the international English language is different in different regions. The same is true when we talk about other languages.

This trend is only gathering pace, but I suppose, later on, we will see the results. What we see today is that mobile traffic and mobile users prevail. We do not always have enough time to use a computer. We use our mobile phones. And, almost all websites and software applications have mobile versions. Unfortunately, when we talk about the tools we use to work, not all of them are mobile-friendly. But I guess step by step the situation will change, and we will be able to use our mobile phones to write technical documentation.

This year has changed a lot. It made a start to so many new things and pushed on so many existing technologies! What we see is that the Internet can help us do almost anything. And, another vital thing is that technical writers and many other professionals should be highly adaptable. New reality – new skills. But I am sure we’ll manage that!

Good luck with your technical writing! ClickHelp Team Author, host and deliver documentation across platforms and devices

## Essential UX Roles: UX Manager, UX Researcher, UX Engineer

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/essential-ux-roles-ux-manager-ux-researcher-ux-engineer/

**Contents:**
- Essential UX Roles: UX Manager, UX Researcher, UX Engineer
- UX Manager
  - Education
  - Requirements
  - Salary
- UX Researcher
  - Education
  - Requirements
  - Salary
- UX Engineer

The domain of User Experience (UX) is extremely broad, encompassing all the experiences a person might have while using a particular product, system, or service. The goal of UX is to design products that provide meaningful and relevant experiences to users. This includes designing the entire lifecycle of a product: branding, design, and usability functions.

UX cannot be overemphasized. Good UX brings satisfaction, leading to increased sales, customer loyalty, and word-of-mouth referrals. Poor UX, on the other hand, frustrates users, wastes time, and ultimately results in lost business.

As the discipline has grown, so too has the complexity and importance of crafting great user experiences. This growth has led to the emergence of many specialized roles within the field, each focusing on different aspects of the UX process. These roles include a UX Manager, who is responsible for overseeing UX projects and strategies; a UX Researcher, who uncovers insights about users’ needs and behaviors; and a UX Engineer, who handles the technical implementation of the design at the user experience level.

In this chapter, we’ll go into each of these areas in detail, exploring the educational background, job requirements, and potential salary ranges for the many career paths user experience has to offer.

The role of a UX Manager is pivotal in steering a product’s design to ensure an optimal user experience. A UX Manager leads and coordinates the efforts of the UX team, setting the vision for the user experience and ensuring it aligns with the company’s goals and user needs. They oversee the entire lifecycle of UX projects, from research and concept development to design implementation and user feedback.

Typically, UX Managers have a strong background in design, psychology, or human-computer interaction. A bachelor’s degree in these fields is common, but many also hold advanced degrees or certifications in UX design or related disciplines. For example, a Master’s degree in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) is highly regarded in the industry.

These are the most critical competencies for a UX manager:

A brilliant UX Manager goes beyond design excellence, as seen in companies like Google or Apple. They demonstrate competence for strategic thinking, with an understanding of the market and business context in which their products operate. This enables them to align the user experience with business goals and user expectations. Additionally, team management is essential, as they are responsible for recruiting, mentoring, and training talent within their teams.

In summary, a UX Manager must be a well-rounded professional, combining expertise in design with strong research, communication, leadership, and strategic skills to lead their teams to success.

The average salary range for a UX Manager varies based on location, company size, and individual experience. The estimated total pay for a User Experience Manager in the United States is $245,656 per year, with an average base salary of $154,741 per year.

A UX manager plays a crucial role in the success of a product’s user experience. Their education, expertise, and leadership skills are essential for creating products that meet user needs and align with business objectives.

A UX Researcher plays a critical role in understanding the behaviors, needs, and motivations of users through various research methods. They are responsible for gathering insights that inform and guide the design process.

A strong educational foundation for a UX Researcher typically includes degrees in fields such as psychology, sociology, anthropology, human-computer interaction (HCI), or design. Certifications in UX research methodologies can also be beneficial. For instance, the Nielsen Norman Group (a leader in the user experience field who trains and certifies UX practitioners) offers specialized courses and certifications that are highly respected in the industry.

The key competencies for a UX Researcher include a robust understanding of qualitative and quantitative research methods, data analysis, and user psychology. Familiarity with tools of the trade such as user testing software (e.g., UsabilityHub, UserTesting.com), analytics tools (e.g., Google Analytics), and survey platforms (e.g., SurveyMonkey) is essential. For example, UX Researchers at companies like Spotify or Airbnb utilize a mix of interviews, surveys, and data analytics to drive design decisions.

The salary for a UX Researcher can vary based on factors such as geographic location, industry, company size, and individual experience. For example, a UX Researcher in the tech industry might earn an average salary ranging from $70,000 to $120,000 in the United States, with variations depending on whether they work for a startup or a large corporation.

In practice, UX Researchers are integral to the design process. At Facebook, for example, UX Researchers might conduct studies to understand how people use social media platforms, which then informs new features or changes to the user interface. Their work ensures that the product development is grounded in real user needs and behaviors.

The role of a UX Engineer intersects the realms of design and engineering. They are responsible for translating design concepts into functional, interactive prototypes and products. Their primary duties include implementing the technical aspects of the user interface (UI), ensuring that designs are feasible, and optimizing the user experience through front-end development.

A UX Engineer typically has a technical education background, with degrees in computer science, web development, or related fields. Specializations in front-end technologies, such as React or Angular, and a strong understanding of UX design principles are highly advantageous.

Technical skills necessary for a UX Engineer include proficiency in HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and knowledge of responsive design techniques. Soft skills such as problem-solving, collaboration, and effective communication are crucial for working closely with UX Designers and other stakeholders.

The salary for a UX Engineer can vary widely depending on experience, location, and the specific industry. Entry-level positions may start at around $100,000, while experienced UX Engineers can earn upwards of $180,000 or more.

The progression outlook for UX Engineers is positive, with opportunities to move into senior roles or specialize further in emerging technologies.

The collaboration between a UX Manager, Researchers, and Engineers forms the foundation for the execution of any user experience initiative. UX Managers take charge of the vision for a UX team, ensuring that projects align to business goals and remain user-centered, while researchers provide the necessary data-driven insights that inform design and technical implementation brought to life via engineering.

In most cases, career progression in UX occurs via promotion from junior to senior positions, with further specialization steps, such as UX Director or Chief Experience Officer. Lateral moves are equally possible, providing professionals with a deeper understanding of various aspects of UX. For example, a UX Designer might transition into a Research role to focus more on user behavior, while an Engineer might move into a managerial role to gain strategic perspectives.

Continuous learning and certification are crucial in the UX field. It is important to stay updated with new trends and technologies. Certifications can be obtained from institutions like the Interaction Design Foundation, one of the largest design educational organizations worldwide, offering online UX design courses to both beginners and professionals. Workshops and conferences also add value to one’s skills. Advanced UX topics available on platforms like Coursera and Udacity can be instrumental in opening up new opportunities and fostering career growth.

There are various roles within UX, all aimed at enhancing user experience. Education in these fields typically includes a mix of technical and design-oriented programs. Success in UX requires technical skills, such as proficiency with design software and coding languages, as well as soft skills like empathy and communication.

Each UX role contributes significantly to user satisfaction and business outcomes. Designers create the visual and interactive elements users engage with. Researchers provide insightful explanations of users’ behaviors and needs, while engineers translate design into functional products. Managers ensure that the UX strategy aligns with business objectives, creating a cohesive and effective user experience that drives engagement and ultimately revenue for the firm.

Good luck with your technical writing!ClickHelp TeamAuthor, host, and deliver documentation across platforms and devices.

## Creating a Fact Sheet: Best Practices for Clear and Engaging Content

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/creating-a-fact-sheet-best-practices/

**Contents:**
- Creating a Fact Sheet: Best Practices for Clear and Engaging Content
- What Is a Fact Sheet?
- Why Is a Fact Sheet Important?
  - Benefits of Fact Sheets
- Creating an Effective Fact Sheet: Steps
  - Step 1: Define Your Purpose
  - Step 2: Identify Your Audience
  - Step 3: Gather Pertinent Data
  - Step 4: Format Your Fact Sheet
  - Step 5: Design for Readability

Speed, clarity, and conciseness are essential for effective communication. Whether presenting data to stakeholders, creating educational materials for students, or promoting a product, a well-structured fact sheet can distill the most important information into a clear and accessible format. This article explains what a fact sheet is, why it matters, and how to create one that captures attention and communicates your message effectively.

A fact sheet is a concise, one- or two-page document that summarizes essential information about a subject, product, or event in a clear and organized format. It serves as a powerful communication tool, allowing readers to quickly grasp vital details without needing to wade through lengthy reports or endure extended presentations. Fact sheets are designed to provide an accessible overview, highlighting key facts and statistics with insights, without overwhelming the reader with unnecessary minutiae.

For instance, imagine an IT company—let’s call it Tech Innovations—has launched a new cybersecurity software solution. To market it effectively, a fact sheet can be created, summarizing the software’s key features, benefits, and pricing.

Key sections to include in the fact sheet:

By distributing this fact sheet to potential customers, industry analysts, and media contacts, Tech Innovations can ensure that the critical aspects of their new product are communicated effectively. Stakeholders can then make well-informed decisions about adopting the solution.

In this way, fact sheets prove to be an invaluable communication resource in the IT industry, enabling companies to distill complex information into a format that is straightforward yet impactful.

Effective communication is the cornerstone of success in today’s fast-paced, information-saturated environment. A fact sheet serves as an excellent tool for presenting ideas and information in a simple, easily digestible format. But why are fact sheets so important? Let’s explore this through a real-life example:

Imagine a software development company, XYZ Inc., is launching a cutting-edge project management tool. To promote this new product, the PR team prepares a press release highlighting its key features and how it addresses common challenges in project management. However, the press release is designed to grab attention and provide a high-level overview.

To complement the press release, the team also creates a fact sheet—a concise, one-page document detailing the product’s key features, benefits, and technical specifications. This fact sheet is shared with journalists and potential customers, ensuring they have easy access to critical details without wading through lengthy materials. By offering this quick-reference guide, XYZ Inc. can effectively capture interest, build credibility, and drive informed decisions, whether by generating media coverage or encouraging potential customers to learn more about the tool.

Fact sheets play a vital role in simplifying complex data into compact, comprehensible formats. They allow organizations to communicate key messages clearly, ensuring readers instantly understand the essential points. By leveraging fact sheets, companies can enhance engagement, foster credibility, and empower well-informed decision-making.

Defining the purpose of your fact sheet is crucial. A fact sheet can serve multiple functions: it might act as a public notice, informing the community about new initiatives or safety information to raise awareness. Alternatively, it could summarize the results of a study, clearly communicating key findings to stakeholders. Another common purpose is promoting a product or service by emphasizing unique features to attract potential customers. By establishing your purpose, you can tailor both the content and design to effectively engage your target audience.

Determine who your fact sheet is intended for. Adjust the language, tone, and complexity of the information to suit your target audience. For example, a fact sheet designed for industry professionals can include technical jargon, while one aimed at the general public should use simplified, accessible language.

Collect all relevant facts, statistics, and information you want to include. Ensure that your data is accurate, up-to-date, and appropriate for your audience. Organize the information into categories or sections that are easy to navigate and understand.

A clear and organized format is critical for effective communication. Here’s a standard structure (or a template) for a fact sheet:

An effectively structured fact sheet guides readers through its main points with ease, ensuring they quickly grasp the key information. Prioritizing clarity and conciseness in the design will enhance comprehension and engagement, maximizing the fact sheet’s impact as a communication tool.

A professional and visually appealing design is essential for readability. Consider the following design tips:

Prioritizing readability through thoughtful design—using clear fonts, proper spacing, and a visually appealing layout—helps capture attention and communicate key messages effectively. A well-designed fact sheet is both engaging and informative, making it a valuable resource for your audience.

Once your fact sheet is drafted, review it carefully for clarity, accuracy, and conciseness. Check for grammatical errors and ensure all information is correctly cited. Seek feedback from colleagues or peers to gain insights and make improvements based on their perspectives.

After finalizing your fact sheet, determine the most effective distribution channels. You can share it digitally—via email, social media, or your website—or print it for distribution at events, conferences, or meetings.

Creating a fact sheet in ClickHelp offers several advantages that enhance the documentation process for any business or organization:

Using ClickHelp to develop a fact sheet not only streamlines the documentation process but also improves the quality and impact of the information you deliver.

The development of an effective fact sheet is a valuable skill that will greatly enhance your communication. You’ll be able to create a clear, engaging, and informative document that communicates your key messages in the most efficient way—whether it’s for informing, educating, or promoting a product. Take the time now to create your fact sheet and see how it can make a difference in conveying your message.

Good luck with your technical writing!

A fact sheet is a concise, one- or two-page document that summarizes essential information about a subject, product, or event in a clear and organized format. It allows readers to quickly grasp key details without wading through lengthy reports or presentations.

Fact sheets are crucial for effective communication, as they simplify complex information into an easily digestible format. They enhance clarity, accessibility, engagement, and professionalism, making them valuable tools for businesses, educators, and media professionals.

Fact sheets are useful for businesses, marketing teams, educators, journalists, government agencies, and nonprofit organizations. They help communicate information effectively to stakeholders, customers, and the general public.

A visually appealing fact sheet includes a clean layout, legible fonts, a clear color scheme, and the right balance of text and visuals. Proper use of white space ensures that the document remains easy to read and engaging.

While a press release is designed to grab media attention with a newsworthy announcement, a fact sheet serves as a supplementary document that provides in-depth details. Using both together can enhance communication and credibility.

## A Guide to Different Types of Manuals in IT

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/types-of-manuals/

**Contents:**
- A Guide to Different Types of Manuals in IT
- What Is a Maintenance Manual?
  - Why Maintenance Manuals Are Irreplaceable
  - How Maintenance Manuals Can Become a Handicap
  - Striking the Right Balance: The Essential Role of Manual Testers
- Understanding User and Instruction Manuals
- The Role of Hardware Manuals in IT
- How Training Manuals Assist Employees
- Specialized IT Manuals: Runbooks, API Documentation, and Others
- Creating Good Manuals

Manuals are a valuable resource in guiding end-users, technicians, and personnel to operate products, perform tasks, and adhere to procedures. This is particularly relevant in IT, where technological complexity demands accurate, well-organized documentation. Understanding the different types of manuals enables organizations to develop tools that simplify product usage, enhance safety, and improve efficiency. This article explores the main categories of manuals through practical examples.

Maintenance manuals provide step-by-step instructions for maintaining and repairing equipment or systems to ensure long-term, reliable operation. They typically include maintenance schedules, troubleshooting instructions, and part replacement procedures.

In IT, maintenance manuals are crucial for data centers and network hardware. For example, a server maintenance guide explains how to clean delicate components like fans and heat sinks without causing overheating, update firmware, replace failing hard drives, and ensure uninterrupted power via routine UPS battery tests. Following these manuals helps IT staff reduce hardware failures and maintain high system availability.

A well-designed maintenance manual consolidates critical information into a single source, eliminating uncertainty and delays. Clear, concise instructions allow technicians to diagnose and resolve issues quickly, reducing downtime and the costs associated with unexpected failures. Such documentation promotes consistency by enforcing standardized procedures across teams, ensuring tasks are performed correctly and safely.

Maintenance manuals are also crucial for safety. They outline proper handling, identify potential hazards, and provide emergency procedures, protecting both personnel and organizations. Moreover, they expedite the training and onboarding of new technicians, enabling them to become productive faster with minimal supervision.

Poorly designed or outdated maintenance manuals can hinder rather than help. Unclear directions, overly complex instructions, or information scattered across multiple documents can confuse technicians and waste time. Manuals that fail to reflect the latest equipment revisions or procedures may lead to improper maintenance, safety risks, or even equipment damage.

Overly rigid adherence to guidelines can stifle professional judgment, particularly in dynamic IT environments where quick troubleshooting is essential. Unless manuals are treated as living documents—regularly updated based on user feedback and operational changes—they risk becoming obsolete and ineffective.

The solution is to create maintenance manuals that are comprehensive yet concise, understandable, and continuously updated. Manual testers, or documentation testers, play a vital role in ensuring quality. They rigorously evaluate manuals to verify clarity, accuracy, and usability before they reach end users.

Manual testers follow instructions step-by-step, acting as typical users to identify inconsistencies, missing information, ambiguous phrasing, or confusing steps. Their feedback helps technical writers and product teams refine manuals for maximum utility. They also test manuals under diverse scenarios, ensuring screenshots match current interfaces, troubleshooting steps cover common issues, and safety warnings are prominent.

Without diligent manual testing, even well-written manuals can fail users, leading to errors, misuse, or costly downtime. Combining well-prepared manuals with thorough testing creates a solid foundation for safe, reliable, and user-friendly documentation.

User manuals, or instruction manuals, target end-users and provide clear, step-by-step guidance for using software or hardware. They are designed to be understandable by individuals with varying technical backgrounds.

A user manual for a cloud storage application guides users through account creation, file uploading and sharing, and troubleshooting issues such as syncing failures. Annotated screenshots help users navigate easily. Similarly, the Ubuntu user guide walks readers through installation, desktop usage, terminal commands, and system configuration — enabling users to get the most from their technology.

Hardware manuals cover the installation, operation, maintenance, and repair of physical IT devices. They ensure users handle equipment correctly and safely.

For instance, a network router manual covers physical setup, from plugging in Ethernet cables to powering the device, accessing the web-based configuration interface, and configuring Wi-Fi and firmware updates. It also provides instructions for resetting the device and includes safety precautions and warranty details. Such manuals empower users to deploy and maintain critical hardware with confidence.

Training manuals are structured resources for employee onboarding, skill development, and ongoing learning. They provide workflows, process descriptions, best practices, and sometimes interactive exercises.

In IT, a helpdesk training manual might include instructions for ticketing system usage, incident classification, escalation procedures, customer interactions, and security protocols for handling sensitive data. Organizations rely on these manuals to standardize service delivery, accelerate new employee training, and ensure operational excellence.

Beyond general manuals, IT professionals use specialized documentation such as runbooks, troubleshooting guides, API documents, and software development kits (SDKs).

Together, these specialized documents enhance productivity, reliability, and collaboration in complex IT environments.

Effective manuals share several key qualities that improve usability and user satisfaction:

Well-crafted manuals improve learning, reduce errors, and enhance product usage while lowering support costs.

Creating good-quality manuals can be tricky, but tools like ClickHelp simplify the entire process with powerful features specifically targeted for technical documentation. ClickHelp allows writers to create, structure, and publish manuals efficiently, whether individually or collaboratively.

One of the main advantages of ClickHelp is its intuitive interface, which allows users with minimal technical skills to create content easily. Contributors can create, edit, and organize manuals using a drag-and-drop environment, focusing on developing clear and useful documentation without dealing with complex software.

AI-powered features further enhance the authoring process. ClickHelp’s intelligent tools assist contributors by fix spelling, change tone, simplify, and the like. For readers, AnswerGenius makes it faster to locate relevant information, reducing time spent navigating large documentation sets. These smart functions help both contributors and end users work more efficiently and accurately.

Collaboration is also straightforward: multiple contributors can work on a single manual. Role-based permissions and commenting enable teams to review each other’s work efficiently, ensuring more accurate and detailed manuals. This allows teams to leverage collective knowledge to produce better content.

ClickHelp offers ready-to-use templates optimized for desktop, tablet, and mobile, making manuals accessible and usable on any platform.

Built-in analytics provide insights into how users interact with manuals—tracking page visits, searches, and most popular content. These data help teams continuously improve and customize manuals for the best user experience.

Additionally, ClickHelp supports conditional content for different user groups or product versions and multi-format publishing (HTML, PDF, EPUB), making manuals versatile and easily distributable.

Overall, ClickHelp reduces the effort required to create, edit, and publish professional, up-to-date manuals for both individual authors and teams.

From data center operations to end-user software guidance, manuals are fundamental to IT success. Clear, well-organized manuals help users, support staff, and developers navigate complex processes, improving efficiency, reliability, and satisfaction. Using the appropriate type of manual, coupled with accurate organization and practical examples, ensures technology professionals and users can perform their roles effectively.

Good luck with your technical writing!

Author, host and deliver documentation across platforms and devices

A maintenance manual provides step-by-step instructions for maintaining and repairing equipment or systems. In IT, it ensures servers, network devices, and other hardware are properly maintained, reducing downtime, preventing failures, and promoting safety.

A user manual is a guide aimed at end users to help them operate software or hardware effectively. An instruction manual is a broader term that may include step-by-step procedures, setup instructions, or usage guidance. In many contexts, they are used interchangeably.

A hardware manual covers installation, operation, maintenance, and repair of physical IT equipment, such as servers, routers, and workstations. It ensures users handle devices safely and correctly.

Training manuals provide structured resources for onboarding, skill development, and ongoing learning. They include workflows, best practices, and step-by-step instructions to help employees quickly become productive while maintaining consistent procedures.

Specialized manuals include runbooks, troubleshooting guides, API documentation, and SDK manuals. These provide detailed operational instructions, coding references, and solutions for common technical problems, improving efficiency and reducing errors in IT operations.

A good manual is clear, logically organized, step-by-step, and uses visual aids to simplify complex concepts. It is written in concise, accessible language, targeted to the intended audience, and updated regularly to remain accurate and useful.

They dont know anything about technical writing, documentation authoring, and of course about online technical writing and documentation tools like ClickHelp product.

We discussed this question with colleagues during a break. Many of us suggested Wikipedia as the resource to get a strict definition of the basic terms in our field. So, what exactly does Wikipedia say about those terms? Let’s take a look…

“Technical writing is a form of technical communication used in a variety of technical and occupational fields, such as computer hardware and software, engineering, chemistry, aeronautics and astronautics, robotics, finance, consumer electronics, and biotechnology.” – see the full article here.

“A technical writer (also called a technical communicator) is a professional writer who engages in technical writing and produces technical documentation.” – see the full article here.

“Online help is topic-oriented, procedural or reference information delivered through computer software. It is a form of user assistance.” – see the full article here.

“Technical communicators use a variety of tools to create usable information. Often referred to as a “tool set”, there is no defined list of tools that are used by all technical writers.” – see the full article here.

## ClickHelp for Startups: Unleash Your Full Potential

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/clickhelp-for-startups-unleash-your-full-potential/

**Contents:**
- ClickHelp for Startups: Unleash Your Full Potential
- Do Startups Need Documentation?

Today, we’re excited to launch the new ClickHelp for Startups Program! In this blog, you’ll discover all the details about this initiative, aimed at providing comprehensive support for startups. Learn how ClickHelp can empower your startup journey with top-notch documentation and invaluable resources.

At the beginning of almost every startup, documentation is rarely at the top of your priority list. Most of the time, your head is spinning from working on features. The cycle of coding, testing, and bug fixing seems never-ending; your team struggles to meet your own deadlines, and sometimes your investor’s milestones loom over you, adding to the tension. Sometimes, you feel that the whole operation is held together by the power of pure will and a few bits of duct tape.

At that stage, proper documentation seems a luxury you can ignore for a while. After all, you have those Google Docs to store all the specifications, references, and the rest – you even sorted them into folders by category! Looks professional (if you squint).

Quick emails solve enough support questions from the users for now (working after hours searching through the cases is perfectly normal at the beginning, right?).

And, of course, you can always resort to Google Docs once again if you need to write a how-to for your audience. You can even make it into a blog post, so they will definitely see it! (It’s okay, you’re used to squinting at this point).

But sooner or later, the lack of clear communication – by means of useful documentation – comes back and bites hard. Support is flooded with all the same questions. The potential user base fades away, discouraged more by the poor in-product guidance and long response time than buggy features. The team drowns in the chaotic sea of scattered docs with lengthy email threads weighing them down.

Is there a way to escape such a fate? Collect your knowledge in a single place so that support engineers don’t waste hours wading through disorganized Google Docs? Have the user manual up and running online for the users to find answers themselves? Don’t make developers wonder if the documentation they read is up to date? Of course.

We’re thrilled to announce our program supporting startups in creating stellar documentation! ClickHelp, an intuitive and powerful authoring tool, is a documentation lifesaver that will help you breathe easily.

Forget wasting your time piecing together homemade wikis or looking for a way to conveniently host online that knowledge base you have in Google Docs. Write directly in ClickHelp — you won’t need much time to adapt since the editor looks quite similar. Plus, you can push the most recent versions of your docs live in just a couple of clicks.

While ClickHelp is a professional solution, it’s easy to use, and it shines in offering a handful of startup-friendly features. Branding your portal, access controls for gated content, or rapid documentation updates tend to be more relevant to a young team than dynamic output, content reuse, or multichannel publishing.

And as your team grows, ClickHelp grows with you. We provide enterprise-grade scalability down the line.

We view documentation as an important and invaluable asset for any startup. And we know all too well that startups tend to realize the need for documentation too late, when the support becomes overwhelmed by users’ cries for help, or when the existence and accessibility of documentation become crucial for your reputation.

## Overcoming Broken Links in Technical Documentation

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/overcoming-broken-links-in-technical-documentation/

**Contents:**
- Overcoming Broken Links in Technical Documentation
- Impact of Links in Technical Documentation
- The Impact of Broken Links on Technical Documentation and SEO
- Resolving Broken Link Issues with ClickHelp
- Optimizing SEO with Strategic Redirects
- Conclusion

Broken links are one of the reasons why SEO optimization may not be effective, leading to a website having a low ranking in search results. Links play a crucial role in SEO optimization as they aid search engines in understanding the relevance and authority of a website.

Links from relevant websites and content are important for establishing the topic and niche of your website. They assist search engines in comprehending the context and relevance of your content. Additionally, backlinks can drive referral traffic to your website, thereby increasing visibility and potentially expanding your customer base.

Internal linking, on the other hand, assists search engines in navigating and understanding the structure of your website. Furthermore, internal links distribute page authority and aid users in discovering more content on your website.

Moreover, anchor text (the clickable text in a hyperlink) provides additional context and relevance to the linked page, helping search engines understand the topic and keywords associated with the link.

In this blog, you will learn how to address the issue of broken links, implement a holistic SEO strategy, and enhance your website’s search engine ranking and visibility.

Links play a crucial role in technical documentation, shaping how users navigate and interact with the content. The significance of links in technical documentation is determined by such factors as their impact on user experience, information retrieval, and overall effectiveness of the documentation.

The importance of links can be summarized in the following list of factors:

In essence, links are pivotal in technical documentation as they enrich content, improve comprehension, and provide access to supplementary information, enhancing the overall effectiveness of the documentation.

Broken links in technical documentation can have a negative impact on user experience and affect search engine optimization (SEO) efforts. When users encounter broken links, it can lead to frustration and difficulty in accessing needed information, resulting in a poor user experience and potential abandonment of the documentation.

From an SEO perspective, broken links can harm the ranking of documentation in search engine results. Search engines interpret broken links as a sign of poor quality or outdated content, which can lower the documentation’s overall ranking.

Technically, when a search engine crawls a site, it indexes content and follows links to other pages to assess relevance and authority. If a page with broken links is crawled, it can negatively affect documentation ranking in several ways:

These factors make it harder for users to discover documentation through search engines, reducing its visibility and accessibility. Regularly checking and maintaining links in technical documentation is essential. This involves updating or removing broken links and implementing redirects when necessary. By doing so, user experience improves, documentation credibility is maintained, and SEO performance is supported.

The link viewer in ClickHelp provides a comprehensive overview of all the links present within a documentation project. It allows users to see a complete list of internal and external links, as well as broken links, within their documentation. The link viewer also offers the capability to filter and search for specific links.

One of the main advantages of the viewer is its ability to identify broken links, which is crucial for maintaining the quality and reliability of the documentation. By detecting broken links, contributors can quickly and efficiently address any issues, ensuring a seamless user experience.

Additionally, the link viewer helps users understand the structure of their documentation by visualizing the interconnectedness of different topics through hyperlinks. This provides valuable insights into the information architecture and helps ensure that the documentation is well-organized and easily navigable.

You can also refresh links using Global Find and Replace in ClickHelp by following these steps:

By following these steps, you can efficiently refresh links across your documentation project.

Redirects are an important tool for improving SEO parameters on a website. Here are some ways redirects can be used to enhance SEO:

By strategically employing redirects, website owners can improve their SEO parameters by preserving link equity, consolidating content, and ensuring a smooth user experience. However, it is important to use redirects judiciously and avoid excessive redirect chains, as they can adversely affect SEO.

In conclusion, broken links in documentation can significantly impact a company’s ability to provide accurate and up-to-date information to its customers and employees. It is crucial for organizations to regularly review and update their documentation to ensure that it remains reliable and accessible. Additionally, implementing a robust system for checking and fixing broken links can help maintain a seamless user experience. By prioritizing the maintenance of documentation and addressing broken links, companies can enhance their credibility and professionalism, ultimately leading to greater customer satisfaction and trust.

## How to Write a Step-by-Step Guide That Anyone Can Follow

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/how-to-write-a-step-by-step-guide/

Effective communication relies on clarity and simplicity. Whether you’re showcasing a new software application, teaching a technical skill, or outlining a complex process, a well-structured step-by-step guide can greatly improve comprehension.

For example, consider the launch of a popular IT product—a cloud storage service—that was released without a comprehensive user manual. Many users became overwhelmed by the interface and features, leading to confusion and frustration. As a result, the company faced a surge of customer support inquiries and negative reviews, highlighting the critical need for clear instructional content. By following the guidelines presented in this article, you’ll be able to avoid similar pitfalls and create a guide that empowers users to navigate your product with confidence.

A step-by-step guide simplifies complex information by breaking it down into manageable parts. This format is beneficial because it ensures the following:

A step-by-step guide is a valuable tool that simplifies even the most complex tasks and boosts efficiency. By breaking down processes into manageable steps, it helps users navigate challenges with confidence. Whether it’s learning new skills, completing projects, or achieving personal goals, a structured approach not only makes the journey smoother but also more successful. These guides empower individuals to tackle even the most daunting tasks with clarity and ease.

Writing a step-by-step guide requires several structured steps. Here is a set of guidelines that one should follow when creating an effective and informative guide:

Example: “How to Set Up a Virtual Private Network (VPN) on Windows”

Example: If your audience comprises beginners, the text should define technical terms and the software which they are used to dealing with.

Example: Instead of saying “The connection should be established,” say “Click ‘Connect’ to establish the VPN connection.”

Example: Add screenshots for each step to show the installation process, the application interface, and connection status.

Here is an example of a step-by-step guide for setting up a Wi-Fi router:

This step-by-step guide for setting up a Wi-Fi router offers several benefits. First, it simplifies the process, making it accessible even for those who may not be tech-savvy. By breaking down the setup into manageable steps, users can follow along easily without feeling overwhelmed.

Additionally, this template serves as a reliable reference, ensuring that important configurations are not overlooked, which can lead to a more stable and secure internet connection.

Finally, having a clear guide boosts user confidence, empowering them to troubleshoot issues and assist others in the future. Overall, this template not only streamlines the setup process but also enhances users’ understanding and control over their home network.

A clear and concise guide can make all the difference when trying to complete new tasks or learn new skills. This step-by-step guide template is designed to provide readers with a structured way of understanding and effectively accomplishing a particular task. Whether it’s setting up a new device, mastering a software application, or learning a new recipe, this guide will simplify the process and improve comprehension.

Introduction: Briefly introduce the topic and explain its significance.

Audience: Identify who the guide is intended for.

Step 1: [Description]

Further details, visuals, or examples if necessary.

Step 2: [Description]

Further details, visuals, or examples if necessary.

Step 3: [Description]

Further details, visuals, or examples if necessary.

Continue as Necessary

Conclusion: Summarize the key takeaways from your guide and let your readers know where to reach out if they have more questions or need clarification.

This how-to guide template will help simplify complex topics, making them more manageable. The step-by-step process will enable readers to better understand and successfully perform the task.

A step-by-step guide is a structured document that breaks down complex tasks into manageable actions. It promotes clarity, reduces ambiguity, and supports learning by providing clear instructions, often accompanied by visuals, to help users accomplish specific goals.

Employees can use these guides to understand processes, tools, or systems more effectively. They serve as reference materials that can enhance productivity and reduce the learning curve for new tasks or software.

People often find step-by-step guides more accessible because they simplify information into sequential actions, making it easier to follow and implement, especially for complex procedures.

## Interactive Documentation: The Future of Engaging Content

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/interactive-documentation-the-future-of-engaging-content/

**Contents:**
- Interactive Documentation: The Future of Engaging Content
- What Is Interactive Documentation?
- Why Interactive Documentation Matters
- How to Make Documentation Interactive
- Examples Across Disciplines
  - Marketing
  - Sales
  - Employee Experience
- Advantages of Interactive Documentation
  - Improved User Experience

Many traditional documentation formats, such as static PDFs, linear Word documents, and simple web pages, no longer meet users’ expectations for engagement. Studies show that typical completion rates for static e-learning courses and MOOCs (Massive Open Online Courses) are less than 25%, suggesting inefficiency in one-size-fits-all linear content delivery. Interactive documentation and dynamic training, however, use bite-sized, engaging content and personalized learning paths, which have been shown to increase completion and engagement rates significantly. Completion rates of 75–85% and retention improvements of up to 50% are possible through interactive means.

Not only does interactive documentation increase completion and comprehension, but it also enhances user satisfaction while reducing the need for additional support. Interactive documents equip users with self-service support, improve product understanding, and drive engagement through multimedia, step-by-step guides, and real-time feedback

Interactive documentation goes beyond static text and images; it’s a living resource that can include videos, audio clips, animations, buttons, embedded forms, and interactive infographics. Users can move through content at their own pace, jump to relevant sections, and participate in quizzes or polls. This format is particularly useful for sales, marketing, HR, training, and customer support teams presenting complex information in a digestible way.

The term “interactive documentation” emerged as static documents became insufficient for modern digital needs. As technology advanced, more participatory content became necessary. It is also called dynamic documentation, living documentation, or smart documentation, reflecting its ability to adapt to user needs. Related concepts include interactive learning, guides, and manuals, all emphasizing user participation and real-time feedback.

Interactive documentation allows engagement, retention, and satisfaction to be tracked.

These points demonstrate that interactive documentation is not a fad but an effective strategy to boost engagement, retention, and insight.

Interactive documentation transforms how teams create and consume content. Best practices include:

Focus on a user-centered experience, not just adding bells and whistles.

Interactive documentation isn’t limited to technical teams. It brings value across many disciplines. These examples show how different departments use interactive content to improve clarity, engagement, and user experience.

Interactive documentation makes the delivery of information more engaging, collaborative, and competitive. Some key benefits include:

Interactive documentation provides users with a richer and more engaging experience because they can easily find and understand the information presented. Clickable diagrams, videos, and interactive tutorials encourage participatory exploration and learning, helping to reduce friction and improve overall satisfaction. According to Forbes, interactive content generates 52.6% more engagement than static content, with users spending an average of 13 minutes interacting with it compared to 8.5 minutes with static content. This demonstrates how effective quizzes, polls, calculators, and other forms of interactive infographics can be. The more digital marketers and content developers adopt these active formats, the more memorable, shareable, and conversion-driving their content becomes.

This means immediate sharing of insights and updates, with teams able to work more effectively. Most interactive documentation solutions enable live editing and instant sharing, supporting seamless communication, accelerating onboarding processes and productivity, and keeping everyone informed with the latest knowledge. For example, teams using collaborative interactive documentation tools have seen significant reductions in onboarding time and increases in knowledge retention; users can access and update content instantly, creating more efficient workflows.

Organizations that adopt interactive documentation stand out from competitors by offering a fresh, user-friendly approach to information sharing. This leads to higher levels of customer and employee satisfaction and positions the organization as an innovator in its field, attracting more users and clients. Companies that implement interactive documentation often report stronger customer satisfaction metrics and increased client acquisition because their content appears more accessible, up-to-date, and engaging.

Interactive documentation provides a real competitive advantage, helping organizations deliver a superior user experience, foster deeper collaboration, and elevate their brand in a crowded market. By embracing interactive elements, teams can create content that delights, informs, and remains memorable—driving meaningful results for both users and businesses.

If you want to experience the power of interactive documentation directly, you can try out interactive API documentation in ClickHelp.

Interactive documentation represents a paradigm shift in organizational communication. By embracing rich media, intelligent design, and user-centered features, teams can create content that educates, informs, and inspires. As interactive documentation becomes the norm, adopters will be better equipped to connect, collaborate, and thrive in a digital world.

Good luck with your technical writing!

Interactive documentation is a modern format of technical or product content that allows users not only to read but also to engage with the material. It may include embedded videos, quizzes, expandable sections, animations, interactive API calls, and other elements that help users learn faster and retain information better.

Traditional documentation is static: users can only scroll and read.Interactive documentation is dynamic: it lets users test, explore, and interact with the product directly inside the documentation. This leads to higher engagement, better comprehension, and reduced support load.

Usually, no. High-quality documentation platforms keep elements synced automatically.For example, with interactive API docs generated from OpenAPI, updates happen automatically when definitions change. Videos, quizzes, and expandable blocks remain intact even as text content is updated.

Indirectly — yes. Although search engines don’t read interactive widgets, they track: longer session duration, lower bounce rate, higher user engagementThese behavioral signals can improve page ranking and overall visibility.

## The Fall of STC: the Profession at the Crossroads

**URL:** https://clickhelp.com/clickhelp-technical-writing-blog/the-fall-of-stc-the-profession-at-the-crossroads/

**Contents:**
- The Fall of STC: the Profession at the Crossroads
- Is Technical Writing Dying?
- Emerging Professions in Technical Communication
  - AI Documentation Specialist
  - Product Experience Writer
  - Technical Content Strategist
  - Localization Technical Writer
- Surviving Communities: Alternatives to STC

To the technical communicators reading this—your profession is not dying. It’s evolving. And with the right tools, the best is yet to come.

In 1953, at the dawn of the technical revolution, a group of forward-thinking professionals gathered in Washington, D.C. to establish the Society for Technical Communication (STC). Born in an era of emerging technical documentation needs—when aerospace, computing, and industrial technologies were rapidly expanding—the STC represented more than just an organization. It was a beacon for professionals transforming complex technical information into clear, accessible language.

For decades, STC stood as the premier organization for technical communicators. Its annual conferences, journals, and certification programs were considered the gold standard. However, the digital revolution would soon challenge its traditional model.

On February 3, 2025, the Society for Technical Communication (STC), a 72-year-old institution, filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy, ceasing operations immediately. The decision didn’t come out of nowhere. One of the reasons was the steady decline in the membership: it dropped by over 30% in a decade, while operational costs (e.g., conferences, certification programs) outpaced revenue. Attempts to cut costs and pivot to digital offerings fell short.

At the same time, STC missed the opportunity to go global. It remained heavily focused on North America, despite the rapid growth of technical communication in Asia, particularly India. Its pricing models, certification requirements, and conference locations were inaccessible to professionals in emerging markets.

For further reading, explore the STC Bankruptcy FAQ or the LinkedIn analysis by Peter Yorke.

The closure sparks reflection: Is this a sign of stagnation in technical communication, or a failure of legacy institutions to evolve?

Well, fortunately, no, the fall of STC doesn’t really mean that. Contrary to fears of obsolescence, the technical writing profession is thriving and actually evolving. There’s about 7% of annual growth in technical writing jobs, with median salary increasing due to specialized skills and expanding opportunities in emerging markets (India, Southeast Asia, Eastern Europe).

The ecosystem is shifting to help connect the people all over the world via decentralized communities, with the accent on remote communication and collaboration. The technical writers now prioritize affordability and flexibility over rigid, membership-driven organizations.

At the same time, the industry calls for specialists to adapt as well, creating new demands such as staying current with advancements in AI and natural language processing or mastering content strategies. Depending on the product, technical writers nowadays often need a certain level of technical proficiency, including coding basics (HTML, Markdown), a clear understanding of AI tools and CMS platforms, and familiarity with UX and design principles. Soft skills — particularly adaptability, cross-cultural collaboration, and teamwork — remain equally critical.

Discover how ClickHelp streamlines content creation, collaboration, and publishing in a remote-friendly, global environment.

This role has emerged to address the need to document AI systems, training data, model deployment, and ethical guidelines.

AI adoption across industries (e.g., healthcare, finance, SaaS) requires clear documentation for developers and end-users. For example, 47% of organizations prioritize AI governance, creating demand for specialists who can translate technical AI processes into accessible content.

Evolution from Technical Writing: Requires skills in AI tool proficiency (e.g., prompt engineering) and data-driven content optimization. AI Documentation Specialists now curate AI-generated drafts, refine outputs for accuracy, and ensure compliance with ethical standards.

Product-led companies (e.g., SaaS platforms) increasingly hire writers to craft in-product content like onboarding guides, tooltips, and error messages that reduce user churn.

As businesses prioritize user retention, the product-led organizations are expanding teams to enhance in-app experiences. Freelance opportunities in this niche are also rising due to its underserved status.

Evolution from Technical Writing: Moves beyond static documentation to dynamic, user-centric content embedded within products. Combines technical clarity with UX writing principles to guide users through workflows.

This role is well-established, with content strategy now recognized as critical for aligning technical documentation with business goals.

The content marketing industry is projected to grow at a 16.01% compound annual growth rate, driving demand for strategists who can manage editorial calendars, SEO, and multi-channel campaigns.

Evolution from Technical Writing: Requires cross-disciplinary skills in data analytics, UX design, and AI tool integration. Technical writers now oversee content ecosystems rather than single documents.

Globalization and multilingual content needs (e.g., for EU compliance) have solidified this role.

Demand: Steady Growth. Companies expanding into international markets require localization experts to adapt technical content culturally and linguistically. Because, even though there are quick and effective AI tools to assist in scaling multilingual content today, human oversight remains critical for nuance.

Evolution from Technical Writing: Builds on traditional technical writing but adds expertise in translation tools (e.g., CAT software) and cultural adaptation.

If you plan to evolve professionally, not only you need to learn and develop new skills in general, but participate in the community. Here are a few of our choices.

The demise of traditional institutions like STC is not a sign of professional extinction, but a clear signal of transformation. Technical writing is becoming more dynamic and more integral to global communication than ever before.

The future belongs to those who can adapt quickly, learn continuously, and blend technical expertise with human creativity.

We at ClickHelp are ready to walk with you through each step of this evolution. As a cloud-based documentation platform built for the new era of technical communication, we offer collaborative tools, AI-powered features, and flexible workflows that are suited not only for traditional technical writing but for these modern specializations as well. Our platform transforms how teams create, manage, and publish technical content—breaking down silos and enabling true global collaboration.

Start your free 14-day trial today and discover how we can empower your documentation team to thrive in the new chapter of technical communication.

Good luck with your technical writing!
